All network sessions are terminated after 15 minutes of inactivity via a combination of firewall and VPN gateway settings. System clocks are synchronized to an internal NTP server (pool.ntp.org) via Group Policy with a synchronization interval of 3600 seconds. Removable media (USB drives, SD cards) is blocked by default on all endpoints using Windows Device Installation Restrictions and BitLocker To Go. Incoming hardware is inspected using a TPM-based attestation tool (e.g., McAfee ePolicy Orchestrator) to verify firmware integrity and detect tampering before deployment.", "technologies": ["CrowdStrike Falcon", "Microsoft Group Policy", "Windows Defender Firewall", "VPN gateway (Palo Alto Networks)", "NTP server (internal)", "BitLocker To Go", "McAfee ePolicy Orchestrator", "TPM attestation"], "configuration_details": "EDR: memory scanning enabled, block suspicious memory regions (e.g., heap spray, ROP chains). Network disconnect: firewall idle timeout 900s, VPN session timeout 15 min. Time sync: NTP server 'time.contoso.com', sync interval 3600s, enabled across domain. Media use: GPO 'Removable Storage Access' set to 'Deny all', BitLocker To Go enforced. Hardware inspection: TPM attestation before device enrollment, firmware hash verification.", "evidence": "EDR reports from CrowdStrike show memory protection alerts and blocks. Firewall logs show session timeout terminations. Time sync logs (w32tm /query /peers) confirm synchronization. GPO results (RSOP) show media restriction policies applied. Hardware inspection logs from McAfee ePO show attestation results for each device.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Memory Protection

mappings.control_title: Network Disconnect

mappings.control_title: System Time Synchronization

mappings.control_title: Media Use

mappings.control_title: Inspection of Systems or Components

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-003", "title": "Public-Facing Web Application Lacks Proper Network Segmentation and Least Functionality Controls", "severity": "high", "description": "The organization's public-facing web application is hosted on a shared internal subnet without logical or physical separation from the internal corporate network. text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Audit Logging and User Accountability Policy", "policy_text": "All information systems must generate and retain audit logs for a minimum of one year to support monitoring and analysis of user activities. text: {"implementation_id": "SC-ARCH-001", "control_area": "Security Architecture and Segmentation", "description": "The enterprise implements a multi-tier network architecture using VLAN segmentation and firewall rules to isolate publicly accessible system components (e.g., web servers, VoIP gateways) into separate DMZ subnets, logically separated from the internal corporate network. VoIP traffic is segmented onto a dedicated VLAN with Quality of Service (QoS) policies, monitored via network access control lists (ACLs) and intrusion detection systems (IDS) to prevent unauthorized use and eavesdropping. All configurations are documented in a central configuration management database (CMDB) and reviewed quarterly.", "technologies": ["Cisco ASA Firewalls", "VMware NSX", "Microsoft Active Directory Group Policy", "Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CallManager)", "Symantec Endpoint Protection", "SolarWinds Network Performance Monitor"], "configuration_details": "DMZ VLANs 10, 20, 30 for web, VoIP, and mobile code testing respectively; firewall rule ID 1050 permits HTTPS from internet to DMZ web servers only; GPO 'MobileCodeRestriction' sets Internet Explorer 'Run ActiveX controls and plug-ins' to Disable; VoIP VLAN 20 configured with QoS DSCP EF (46); ACL on core switch denies all non-VoIP traffic to VLAN 20 except from authorized call managers.", "evidence": "Quarterly network segmentation review reports; GPO report output showing 'MobileCodeRestriction' applied to all domain-joined systems; firewall rule audit logs showing only allowed traffic to DMZ; VoIP call detail records (CDR) reviewed monthly for anomalies.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Control and monitor the use of mobile code.

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "System Change and Configuration Management Policy", "policy_text": "All changes to organizational systems, including hardware, software, firmware, and network configurations, must be formally defined, documented, approved, and enforced through a change management process. text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Unauthorized Software Installation and Remote Access via Non-Approved Telecommunications", "severity": "high", "description": "During a review of endpoint configurations and network logs, it was discovered that multiple employees in high-risk positions (e.g., system administrators) had installed unauthorized third-party remote access software on their workstations. The lack of memory protection controls allowed the software to execute arbitrary code, and the telecommunications services used were not approved or backed by alternate service agreements.", "control_gaps": ["No technical controls to prevent unauthorized software installation or execution from memory (SI-16)", "No enforcement of software usage restrictions for remote access tools (CM-10)", "Lack of approved and resilient telecommunications services for remote access; use of unapproved third-party services (CP-8)", "Failure to designate and monitor high-risk positions for enhanced security controls (PS-2)"], "remediation": "Implement application whitelisting and memory protection controls (e.g., Data Execution Prevention, code integrity policies) to block unauthorized software execution. Enforce software usage policies via endpoint management tools and ensure all remote access tools are pre-approved. Establish formal agreements with approved telecommunications providers and maintain alternate services. Review and update position risk designations to apply stricter monitoring and access controls for roles with elevated privileges."}

mappings.control_title: Memory Protection
mappings.reasoning: The finding describes unauthorized code execution via remote access software, which directly relates to SI-16's requirement to protect memory from unauthorized execution. The lack of memory protection controls was identified as a gap.

mappings.control_title: Software Usage Restrictions
mappings.reasoning: The finding explicitly states that the installed remote access software was unauthorized and not covered by software usage agreements, mapping to CM-10's requirement to restrict software use to approved and contracted software.

mappings.control_title: Telecommunications Services
mappings.reasoning: The finding mentions use of non-approved telecommunications services (third-party remote access) without alternate service agreements, which maps to CP-8's requirement for establishing alternate telecommunications services with appropriate agreements.

mappings.control_title: Position Risk Designation
mappings.reasoning: The finding highlights that employees in high-risk positions (system administrators) were involved, and the control gap includes failure to designate and monitor such positions, directly relating to PS-2's requirement for position risk designation.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-003", "risk_statement": "A critical internal application uses persistent, mutable virtual machine instances for all user sessions. mappings.control_title: Non-Persistence

mappings.control_title: Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity
mappings.reasoning: The risk describes potential unauthorized changes to the OS and application stack, and the treatment calls for integrity verification mechanisms (e.g., file integrity monitoring, hash validation) to detect tampering. mappings.control_title: Policy and Procedures
mappings.reasoning: The treatment explicitly recommends developing formal system integrity policies and procedures to govern the use of non-persistent environments and integrity checks. text: {"implementation_id": "impl-enterprise-001", "control_area": "Centralized Security Management and Contingency Planning", "description": "The organization utilizes a centralized security information and event management (SIEM) platform, Splunk Enterprise Security (ES), to aggregate, monitor, and manage security controls across all on-premises and cloud environments. Contingency plans are documented and stored in a centralized Confluence space, with automated backup and recovery procedures tested quarterly via Ansible playbooks that restore critical systems to predefined recovery point objectives (RPO of 4 hours) and recovery time objectives (RTO of 24 hours). Risk response actions from assessments and continuous monitoring are tracked in a Jira project management system, where findings are automatically assigned to system owners for remediation, with SLAs enforced via ServiceNow integration. Information sharing between departments (e.g., HR and IT) is governed by data classification labels applied using Microsoft Purview, with sharing rules enforced through Azure Active Directory conditional access policies. Training and maintenance policies are authored in a centralized policy management tool (PolicyTech), distributed via the company's learning management system (Cornerstone), and reviewed annually. Procedures for maintenance (e.g., patch management) are automated using SCCM and Ansible, with logs sent to Splunk for audit evidence.", "technologies": ["Splunk Enterprise Security", "Confluence", "Ansible", "Jira", "ServiceNow", "Microsoft Purview", "Azure Active Directory", "PolicyTech", "Cornerstone LMS", "SCCM"], "configuration_details": "Splunk ES is configured with 500+ correlation rules covering all NIST controls; contingency plan documents in Confluence are version-controlled and require two approvals for changes; Ansible playbooks for DR include automated failover to AWS us-east-1; Jira projects have custom workflows with mandatory risk response fields and 30-day SLA for critical findings; Microsoft Purview labels are auto-applied to documents containing PII, and sharing is blocked for 'Confidential' labels unless explicitly approved; PolicyTech requires annual attestation for all policies; SCCM is configured for monthly patching with automatic deployment to test groups first.", "evidence": "Splunk dashboards showing real-time alerting and compliance coverage; Confluence page version history with last review date; Ansible playbook execution logs and post-restoration test reports; Jira issue history with resolution timestamps; Microsoft Purview activity logs for data sharing events; PolicyTech attestation reports; SCCM compliance reports showing patch status per system.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Central Management
mappings.reasoning: The implementation describes centralized management of security controls via Splunk ES, Confluence, Jira, and PolicyTech, directly addressing the requirement to centrally manage security controls across the enterprise.

mappings.control_title: Contingency Plan
mappings.reasoning: The description includes a documented contingency plan in Confluence with defined RPO (4 hours) and RTO (24 hours), quarterly testing via Ansible, and automated recovery procedures, meeting the core CP-2 requirements.

mappings.control_title: Risk Response
mappings.reasoning: Risk responses from assessments and monitoring are tracked in Jira with SLAs enforced via ServiceNow, which aligns with RA-7's requirement to respond to findings from assessments and continuous monitoring.

mappings.control_title: Information Sharing
mappings.reasoning: The use of Microsoft Purview labels and Azure AD conditional access policies to govern information sharing based on data classification and defined circumstances directly maps to AC-21.

mappings.control_title: Policy and Procedures (Awareness and Training)
mappings.reasoning: Training policies are developed in PolicyTech, disseminated via Cornerstone LMS, and reviewed annually, satisfying the AT-1 requirement for developing, documenting, and disseminating awareness and training policy and procedures.

mappings.control_title: Policy and Procedures (Maintenance)
mappings.reasoning: Maintenance policies are authored in PolicyTech with automated patch management via SCCM and Ansible, and logs sent to Splunk, covering the MA-1 requirement for maintenance policy and procedures.

mappings.control_title: Configuration Change Control

mappings.control_title: Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Remote Access and Data Protection Policy", "policy_text": "All remote access sessions to the corporate network must be monitored and logged in real-time, with session activity reviewed weekly for unauthorized changes or data exfiltration. Backup data containing controlled unclassified information (CUI) must be encrypted at rest using FIPS 140-2 validated encryption and stored in approved, physically secure facilities. Email and messaging systems must employ automated spam filtering mechanisms that block malicious attachments and phishing links before delivery to end users. Any remote connection must be established through an approved VPN with multi-factor authentication, and all backup storage locations must be access-controlled and audited quarterly.", "department": "Information Security", "classification": "confidential"}

mappings.control_title: Employ spam protection mechanisms
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly requires automated spam filtering to block malicious content in email and messaging systems, directly aligning with SI.L2-3.14.7's requirement for spam protection.

mappings.control_title: Protect the confidentiality of backup CUI at storage locations
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates encryption at rest for backup CUI using FIPS 140-2 validated encryption and secure storage facilities, directly addressing MP.L2-3.8.9's requirement to protect backup CUI confidentiality.

mappings.control_title: Monitor and control remote access sessions
mappings.reasoning: The policy requires real-time monitoring and logging of remote access sessions, weekly reviews, and VPN with MFA, which maps to AC.L2-3.1.12's mandate to monitor and control remote access sessions.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-MP-2025-001", "risk_statement": "A lack of consistent media sanitization procedures and insufficient access controls on storage media could lead to unauthorized recovery of sensitive data from decommissioned hard drives and tapes, resulting in a data breach and regulatory penalties.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement and enforce a formal media sanitization policy that includes secure erasure, degaussing, and physical destruction for all media leaving organizational control. mappings.reasoning: The risk directly addresses the lack of consistent media sanitization procedures, which is the core focus of MP-6. The recommended treatment includes secure erasure and physical destruction, aligning with the control's requirement to sanitize media prior to disposal or release.

mappings.control_title: Media Access
mappings.reasoning: The risk mentions insufficient access controls on storage media, which is directly addressed by MP-2. The treatment includes restricting physical and logical access to media storage areas, matching the control's objective to limit access to authorized individuals.

mappings.control_title: Access Enforcement

text: {"implementation_id": "IMP-MA-001", "control_area": "System Maintenance \u2013 Tool, Media, and Off-site Controls", "description": "The organization maintains a central maintenance tool repository where all diagnostic and test programs are stored after approval and scanning. Microsoft Defender: scheduled scan policy set to scan removable media on connect with 'Full Scan' and 'Remediate threats automatically.' Splunk: correlation rule 'Media_Sanitization_Failure' triggered when sanitization certificate not uploaded within 1 hour of asset decommission.", "evidence": "Audit logs from ServiceNow showing tool checkout assignments; Defender scan logs showing media scans with 'No threats found' status; Blancco sanitization certificates with serial numbers and verification hashes; SIEM reports correlating maintenance tasks with sanitization events.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Content of Audit Records

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "PII Processing and Consent Policy", "policy_text": "All processing of personally identifiable information (PII) must be conducted solely for the purposes explicitly documented in the official processing register and communicated to data subjects. mappings.control_title: Consent

mappings.control_title: Authority to Process PII

mappings.control_title: Notice

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Unsecured Default Configurations and Idle User Accounts in Development Environment", "severity": "high", "description": "During the audit of the development environment, it was observed that several virtual machines were deployed using a default base image without applying organization-specific security baselines. text: {"implementation_id": "impl-enterprise-sysarch-001", "control_area": "System and Communications Protection, Configuration Management, Maintenance", "description": "The enterprise deploys a layered network architecture with an internal management VLAN (VLAN 400) logically separated from user-facing production subnets. All administrative access to network devices, servers, and security appliances requires SSH key-based authentication plus a TOTP token (multifactor authentication) even for out-of-band maintenance sessions routed through a VPN. Change management is enforced via a GitOps pipeline (GitLab CI) that triggers automated security impact analysis (Tenable.io scan) on any configuration or code change before deployment. Shared resources like hypervisors enforce strict VM isolation using vSphere resource pools and NSX micro-segmentation to prevent lateral data leakage. Public-facing web servers reside in a DMZ subnet (VLAN 100) with firewall rules that block all initiated outbound traffic to internal networks except through a managed jump box.", "technologies": ["Cisco Catalyst 9300 switches (VLAN 400 management)", "Fortinet FortiGate 600E firewall (DMZ segmentation)", "VMware vSphere 8 with NSX-T (micro-segmentation)", "GitLab CI/CD pipeline", "Tenable.io (vulnerability scanning for change impact analysis)", "Duo Security (MFA for admin/maintenance sessions)", "OpenVPN (out-of-band maintenance VPN)"], "configuration_details": "Management VLAN 400 configured with ACL allowing SSH/HTTPS only from dedicated admin jump hosts. DMZ VLAN 100 has stateful inspection rule 'deny ip any 10.0.0.0/8' to prevent data exfiltration. NSX distributed firewall rules enforce zero-trust between application tiers (web, app, db). GitLab CI pipeline includes a 'security-impact' job that runs a Tenable.io scan against the staging environment and fails if critical vulnerabilities are detected. Duo authentication proxy configured to require push approval for all SSH sessions to network gear. OpenVPN server uses client certificates + Duo MFA for nonlocal maintenance.", "evidence": "Network diagrams showing VLAN segmentation and firewall rulesets. GitLab merge request logs with attached Tenable.io scan reports. Duo authentication logs showing MFA usage for admin sessions. NSX security policy reports confirming micro-segmentation rules. Change management tickets referencing security impact analysis results.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: SC.L2-3.13.3 — Separate user functionality from system management functionality.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation uses a dedicated management VLAN (VLAN 400) with access control lists that restrict administrative access to specific jump hosts, effectively separating user-facing network traffic from system management functions.

mappings.control_title: CM.L2-3.4.4 — Analyze the security impact of changes prior to implementation.
mappings.reasoning: The GitLab CI pipeline enforces automated security impact analysis via Tenable.io scanning before any change is deployed, and merge requests require scan results to be attached, demonstrating analysis prior to implementation.

mappings.control_title: SC.L2-3.13.4 — Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.
mappings.reasoning: VMware NSX micro-segmentation and distributed firewall rules enforce isolation between virtualized application tiers, preventing lateral movement and unauthorized data transfer across shared hypervisor resources.

mappings.control_title: MA.L2-3.7.5 — Require multifactor authentication to establish nonlocal maintenance sessions via external network connections.
mappings.reasoning: Nonlocal maintenance sessions are established through an OpenVPN gateway that requires both client certificate authentication and Duo MFA push approval, satisfying the multifactor authentication requirement.

mappings.control_title: SC.L2-3.13.5 — Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks.
mappings.reasoning: Public-facing web servers are placed in a DMZ VLAN (VLAN 100) with firewall rules that block direct outbound traffic to internal networks, achieving logical separation of publicly accessible components.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-SI-001", "title": "Lack of Automated Spam and Malware Protection on Email and Endpoints", "current_state": "The organization uses basic antivirus software on some workstations but lacks centralized email filtering or spam protection. Security alerts from external sources are reviewed manually on an ad-hoc basis, with no automated response mechanisms in place.", "target_state": "Spam protection mechanisms must be employed at email gateways and endpoints to block malicious or unwanted messages. Malicious code protection must be deployed and maintained at all designated system entry and exit points. A process must be established to monitor security alerts and advisories and take timely, documented actions in response.", "gap_description": "The current state lacks automated spam filtering and centralized malicious code protection, and has no formal process to monitor and respond to security alerts and advisories, creating significant risk of successful phishing, malware infection, and delayed incident response.", "recommended_actions": ["Deploy an email security gateway with spam and malware filtering capabilities.", "Install and centrally manage endpoint protection platforms (EPP) on all workstations and servers.", "Establish a subscription to relevant security alert and advisory feeds (e.g., US-CERT, vendor bulletins).", "Implement a security information and event management (SIEM) system to automate alert monitoring and response workflows.", "Define and document standard operating procedures for triaging and responding to security alerts and advisories.", "Conduct training for IT staff on the new tools and response procedures."], "priority": "high", "estimated_effort": "weeks"}

mappings.control_title: Employ spam protection mechanisms.
mappings.reasoning: The gap identifies lack of email filtering and spam protection, which directly maps to CMMC SI.L2-3.14.7 requiring spam protection mechanisms.

mappings.control_title: Provide protection from malicious code at designated locations within organizational systems.
mappings.reasoning: The gap describes basic antivirus on some workstations but no centralized malicious code protection at gateways, mapping to CMMC SI.L2-3.14.2's requirement for malicious code protection at designated locations.

mappings.control_title: Monitor system security alerts and advisories and take appropriate actions in response.
mappings.reasoning: The gap mentions ad-hoc manual review of alerts with no automated response, mapping to CMMC SI.L2-3.14.3 requiring monitoring of security alerts and advisories and appropriate actions.

mappings.control_title: Spam Protection
mappings.reasoning: CMMC SI.L2-3.14.7 is derived from NIST SP 800-53 SI-8, so the gap maps to SI-8 requiring spam protection mechanisms at designated information system entry and exit points.

mappings.control_title: Malicious Code Protection
mappings.reasoning: CMMC SI.L2-3.14.2 maps to NIST SP 800-53 SI-3, which requires malicious code protection mechanisms at designated locations within organizational systems.

mappings.control_title: Security Alerts, Advisories, and Directives
mappings.reasoning: CMMC SI.L2-3.14.3 maps to NIST SP 800-53 SI-5, which requires receiving and responding to security alerts, advisories, and directives from external organizations.

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Incident Response and Data Integrity Policy", "policy_text": "All incidents must be declared within one hour of detection based on predefined criteria, with comprehensive metadata and raw data collected and preserved in a tamper-evident log. Physical security monitoring systems must be operational 24/7 to detect unauthorized access or environmental anomalies. Public communications regarding incident recovery shall be issued only through authorized corporate channels and approved messaging templates. Supplier terminations require a formal disposition plan for all associated data, services, and products, executed within 30 days. Software assets must be maintained with critical patches applied within 48 hours, and end-of-life software replaced within 90 days of risk assessment. All incident data must maintain integrity and provenance through cryptographic hashing and chain-of-custody documentation.", "department": "Corporate Security and Compliance", "classification": "confidential"}

mappings.control_title: Incident data and metadata are collected, and their integrity and provenance are preserved.
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly requires comprehensive metadata and raw data collection with cryptographic hashing and chain-of-custody to preserve integrity and provenance.

mappings.control_title: Incidents are declared when adverse events meet the defined incident criteria.
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates declaration within one hour based on predefined criteria, directly addressing the declaration requirement.

mappings.control_title: The physical environment is monitored to find potentially adverse events.
mappings.reasoning: The policy requires 24/7 physical security monitoring for unauthorized access and environmental anomalies, aligning with physical environment monitoring.

mappings.control_title: Public updates on incident recovery are shared using approved messaging and methods.
mappings.reasoning: The policy restricts public incident recovery communications to authorized channels and approved templates, matching the control's mandate.

mappings.control_title: Cybersecurity supply chain risk management plans include provisions for determining a disposition for data, services, and products when the relationship with a supplier is terminated.
mappings.reasoning: The policy requires a formal disposition plan for data, services, and products within 30 days of supplier termination, directly mapping to the control.

mappings.control_title: Software is maintained and replaced in accordance with risk.
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates critical patch application within 48 hours and end-of-life replacement within 90 days based on risk assessment, aligning with risk-based maintenance and replacement.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Unrestricted Remote Access and Session Management Deficiencies for Privileged Functions", "severity": "high", "description": "Remote access to the administrative interface is not routed through a managed access control point, allowing direct connections from external networks. Additionally, user sessions for privileged accounts are not automatically terminated after inactivity, and there is no functional restriction limiting these accounts to only authorized transactions or functions. This creates a risk of unauthorized access, session hijacking, and misuse of elevated privileges.", "control_gaps": ["Remote access connections bypass managed access control points (e.g., VPN, bastion host).", "Privileged user sessions lack automatic termination after a defined period of inactivity.", "No enforcement of least privilege restricting privileged accounts to specific permitted transactions and functions."], "remediation": "Route all remote administrative access through a managed access control point such as a VPN or jump server with multi-factor authentication. Implement automatic session timeout for all user sessions, especially privileged accounts, after 15 minutes of inactivity. Configure role-based access controls to limit privileged accounts to only the specific functions and transactions required for their duties."}

mappings.control_title: Limit system access to the types of transactions and functions that authorized users are permitted to execute.
mappings.reasoning: The finding describes that privileged accounts are not restricted to only authorized transactions and functions, directly mapping to AC.L2-3.1.2 which requires limiting access to permitted types of transactions and functions.

mappings.control_title: Terminate (automatically) a user session after a defined condition.
mappings.reasoning: The finding identifies that privileged user sessions are not automatically terminated after inactivity, which directly maps to AC.L2-3.1.11 requiring automatic session termination after a defined condition such as inactivity.

mappings.control_title: Route remote access via managed access control points.
mappings.reasoning: The finding explicitly states that remote access to the administrative interface is not routed through a managed access control point, which directly maps to AC.L2-3.1.14 requiring remote access to be routed via managed access control points.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-MEDIA-003", "risk_statement": "Unencrypted removable media (USB drives) containing sensitive customer data are used by employees without authorization, increasing the risk of data exfiltration or loss if a device is misplaced or stolen.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement technical controls to enforce encryption on all removable media, restrict the use of unauthorized USB devices via endpoint management tools, and establish a physical media storage policy requiring locked cabinets for any media containing sensitive data."}

mappings.control_title: Media Storage
mappings.reasoning: The risk involves physical loss of removable media containing sensitive data. MP-4 requires physically controlling and securely storing digital and non-digital media, which directly addresses the need for locked storage and access controls for USB drives.

mappings.control_title: Media Use
mappings.reasoning: The risk involves unauthorized use of USB drives. MP-7 requires restricting or prohibiting the use of certain types of media on systems, which maps to the need for policies and technical controls that block unauthorized removable media.

mappings.control_title: Media Use
mappings.reasoning: The risk also implies a need to enforce encryption on allowed media. MP-7's requirement to restrict or prohibit use of media types can be extended to mandate encryption as a condition of use, ensuring that even permitted media are protected against data exposure.

text: {"implementation_id": "IA-AUTH-IMP-2024-001", "control_area": "Identification and Authentication (Non-Organizational Users)", "description": "The organization implements a multi-factor authentication (MFA) system for all non-organizational users, including contractors and partners, through an identity provider (IdP) that integrates with the enterprise directory. Users must authenticate using a combination of a password and a time-based one-time password (TOTP) from an authenticator app. Re-authentication is enforced for sessions idle for more than 15 minutes or when accessing sensitive resources, such as financial systems or personal data repositories. Identity proofing is handled via a third-party identity verification service that validates government-issued IDs and cross-references against commercial databases before account provisioning. All authentication events are logged in a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system for audit and compliance review.", "technologies": ["Okta Identity Cloud", "Microsoft Active Directory (hybrid)", "Duo Security for MFA", "Veriff for identity proofing", "Splunk SIEM"], "configuration_details": "MFA policy: require TOTP for all non-organizational users; re-authentication timeout set to 900 seconds (15 minutes) via IdP session policies; identity proofing requires document upload and liveness check; proofing results stored in HR system with retention of 5 years.", "evidence": "Audit logs from Okta showing MFA events and re-authentication triggers for non-organizational users; identity proofing completion certificates from Veriff; SIEM alerts for failed MFA attempts; quarterly access review reports.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Identification and Authentication (Non-Organizational Users)
mappings.reasoning: The implementation explicitly addresses non-organizational users (contractors, partners) with unique identification through the IdP and MFA for authentication, matching IA-8 requirements for unique identification and authentication of such users.

mappings.control_title: Re-Authentication
mappings.reasoning: The description includes a 15-minute idle session re-authentication policy and re-authentication for sensitive resource access, which directly corresponds to IA-11's requirement to re-authenticate under defined circumstances.

mappings.control_title: Identity Proofing
mappings.reasoning: The use of an identity verification service (Veriff) for government ID validation and database cross-referencing before account provisioning fulfills IA-12's identity proofing requirements for logical access.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-IA-2024-001", "title": "Insufficient Authentication Controls for Privileged Access", "current_state": "The organization currently uses single-factor password authentication for all local and network access to privileged accounts. Passwords are set with minimal complexity requirements (e.g., 6 characters, no special characters required) and are changed only annually. No multifactor authentication mechanisms are in place, and the authentication protocol used for network access does not include replay-resistant measures such as timestamps or nonces.", "target_state": "Multifactor authentication must be implemented for all local and network access to privileged accounts, as well as for network access to non-privileged accounts. Replay-resistant authentication mechanisms (e.g., Kerberos, time-based tokens) must be employed for all network access. Additionally, password policies must enforce minimum complexity (e.g., at least 12 characters, including uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters) and require a minimum change of characters when passwords are reset.", "gap_description": "The absence of MFA and replay-resistant mechanisms for privileged accounts exposes the organization to credential theft and replay attacks. Weak password complexity and infrequent password changes further increase the risk of unauthorized access.", "recommended_actions": ["Deploy an MFA solution (e.g., hardware tokens, authenticator app) for all privileged account logins, both local and network-based.", "Configure network authentication protocols (e.g., Kerberos, NTLMv2) to enforce replay resistance by enabling time-based or challenge-response mechanisms.", "Update password policy to require a minimum of 12 characters with at least one uppercase letter, one lowercase letter, one number, and one special character.", "Implement a password history policy that prevents reuse of the last 10 passwords and enforces a minimum of 4 character changes when creating new passwords.", "Conduct a pilot test with the IT administration team to validate MFA integration with existing systems (e.g., Windows Active Directory, Linux sudoers).", "Provide training to privileged users on new authentication procedures and password creation requirements."], "priority": "critical", "estimated_effort": "weeks"}

mappings.control_title: Use multifactor authentication for local and network access to privileged accounts and for network access to non-privileged accounts.
mappings.reasoning: The gap explicitly identifies the lack of MFA for privileged accounts and network access to non-privileged accounts, directly mapping to the requirement in IA.L2-3.5.3.

mappings.control_title: Employ replay-resistant authentication mechanisms for network access to privileged and non-privileged accounts.
mappings.reasoning: The current state lacks replay-resistant measures (e.g., timestamps, nonces) for network authentication, which is a direct violation of IA.L2-3.5.4.

mappings.control_title: Enforce a minimum password complexity and change of characters when new passwords are created.
mappings.reasoning: The weak password complexity and lack of character change requirements in the current state directly map to the requirements of IA.L2-3.5.7.

mappings.control_title: Identification and Authentication (Organizational Users)
mappings.reasoning: The overall gap in authentication mechanisms for privileged users aligns with NIST IA-2, which requires unique identification and authentication for organizational users, including MFA for privileged access.

mappings.control_title: Authenticator Management
mappings.reasoning: The weak password policy and lack of MFA relate to IA-5 requirements for managing authenticators, including password complexity, history, and MFA implementation.

mappings.control_title: Device Identification and Authentication
mappings.reasoning: While the gap focuses on user authentication, the lack of replay-resistant mechanisms also impacts device authentication in network access, partially mapping to IA-3.

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Personnel Security and Access Termination Policy", "policy_text": "All organizational positions, including those filled by contractors and temporary staff, must be assigned a risk designation based on the sensitivity of information accessed and the criticality of systems operated. Upon separation from the organization, whether voluntary or involuntary, all access credentials, badges, and system accounts must be revoked within 24 hours. External personnel, such as consultants and service providers, must adhere to the same personnel security requirements as internal employees, including background checks and access termination procedures. Failure to comply with these requirements may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination of contract.", "department": "Human Resources", "classification": "confidential"}

mappings.control_title: Personnel Termination
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly requires revocation of all access credentials and system accounts within 24 hours of personnel separation, directly addressing the termination of information system access upon termination of employment.

mappings.control_title: Position Risk Designation
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates that all organizational positions be assigned a risk designation based on sensitivity and criticality, which aligns with the requirement to assign risk designations to positions.

mappings.control_title: External Personnel Security
mappings.reasoning: The policy states that external personnel must meet the same security requirements as internal employees, including background checks and access termination, which corresponds to ensuring external providers meet organizational personnel security requirements.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Unsecured CUI on Public-Facing Web Server During Employee Offboarding", "severity": "high", "description": "A public-facing web server was discovered hosting CUI documents in an unprotected directory accessible without authentication. The server had been used by a recently terminated employee who retained administrative access credentials post-termination. No cryptographic protections were applied to the stored CUI, and the server had not been included in the latest quarterly security control assessment.", "control_gaps": ["CUI posted or processed on publicly accessible systems without access controls", "Lack of cryptographic protection for CUI stored on digital media during transport or at rest", "Failure to revoke system access for terminated personnel in a timely manner", "Incomplete periodic assessment of security controls for systems processing CUI"], "remediation": "Immediately remove the CUI from the public directory, implement authentication and authorization controls for the web server, and apply encryption to all CUI at rest. Conduct a full review of access rights for all former employees and schedule a comprehensive security control assessment for the server within 30 days."}

mappings.control_title: AC.L2-3.1.22 — Control CUI posted or processed on publicly accessible systems.
mappings.reasoning: The finding describes CUI documents hosted on a public-facing web server without access controls, directly violating the requirement to control CUI on publicly accessible systems.

mappings.control_title: MP.L2-3.8.6 — Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of CUI stored on digital media during transport.
mappings.reasoning: The CUI stored on the web server's digital media (hard drive) lacked encryption, failing to protect confidentiality during any potential transport or unauthorized access.

mappings.control_title: PS.L2-3.9.2 — Ensure that organizational systems containing CUI are protected during and after personnel actions such as terminations and transfers.
mappings.reasoning: The terminated employee retained administrative access, indicating a failure to protect the system containing CUI after the personnel action of termination.

mappings.control_title: CA.L2-3.12.1 — Periodically assess the security controls to determine if the controls are effective in their application.
mappings.reasoning: The server was not included in the latest quarterly assessment, demonstrating a gap in periodic evaluation of security controls for a system processing CUI.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-0042", "risk_statement": "Failure to regularly test the contingency plan, combined with weak cryptographic controls and inconsistent identifier management, could lead to prolonged system outages during a disaster and unauthorized access to sensitive data. Additionally, the lack of synchronized time stamps across audit logs hampers forensic analysis, and the absence of centralized security control management increases the risk of misconfigurations.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement a quarterly contingency plan testing schedule with documented results, enforce cryptographic standards for all data at rest and in transit, deploy a centralized identity management system with unique identifiers, synchronize all system clocks to an authoritative time source, and establish a centralized security operations team to oversee control implementation."}

mappings.control_title: Contingency Plan Testing
mappings.reasoning: The risk statement explicitly mentions failure to regularly test the contingency plan, which directly maps to CP-4 requiring testing to determine effectiveness.

mappings.control_title: Cryptographic Protection
mappings.reasoning: The risk highlights weak cryptographic controls leading to unauthorized access, aligning with SC-13's requirement to determine and document cryptographic protection.

mappings.control_title: Identifier Management
mappings.reasoning: Inconsistent identifier management is cited as a contributing factor, which maps to IA-4's requirement to manage system identifiers through authorization and tracking.

mappings.control_title: Time Stamps
mappings.reasoning: The lack of synchronized time stamps across audit logs is explicitly mentioned, directly mapping to AU-8's requirement for internal system clocks to generate time stamps.

mappings.control_title: Central Management
mappings.reasoning: The absence of centralized security control management is noted as increasing misconfiguration risk, mapping to PL-9's requirement to centralize management of security controls.

text: {"implementation_id": "IMP-2024-045", "control_area": "Access Control and Recovery Planning", "description": "The organization implements a role-based access control (RBAC) system managed via Azure Active Directory (Azure AD) with privileged identity management (PIM) for just-in-time access. The system enforces least privilege by assigning granular permissions based on job functions and requires dual authorization for all privileged operations. Post-incident recovery actions are documented in the Incident Response Plan (IRP) and executed via automated playbooks in Splunk SOAR, which prioritize critical business functions for restoration. Recovery plans are tested quarterly through tabletop exercises, with findings reviewed by the CISO and reported to the executive leadership team for continuous improvement.", "technologies": ["Azure Active Directory", "Azure Privileged Identity Management", "Splunk SOAR", "ServiceNow ITAM", "Jira for incident tracking"], "configuration_details": "Azure AD conditional access policies enforce MFA for all privileged roles. PIM roles are activated for a maximum of 4 hours with approval from a separate security manager. Splunk SOAR playbooks define recovery steps for ransomware, data breach, and system outage scenarios, with dependencies on critical applications mapped in ServiceNow. Separation of duties is enforced via Azure AD administrative units: no single user has both read and write permissions on financial systems.", "evidence": "Quarterly access review reports from Azure AD show 100% compliance with least privilege policies. Splunk SOAR execution logs demonstrate recovery playbook runs within 30 minutes of incident trigger. Incident post-mortem documents from Jira include recommendations to leadership and show closure within 7 days. Annual tabletop exercise reports are signed by the CISO.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Access permissions, entitlements, and authorizations are defined in a policy, managed, enforced, and reviewed, and incorporate the principles of least privilege and separation of duties.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation describes RBAC via Azure AD with PIM for least privilege, dual authorization, and quarterly access reviews, directly addressing the management and review of permissions and separation of duties.

mappings.control_title: Critical mission functions and cybersecurity risk management are considered to establish post-incident operational norms.
mappings.reasoning: The Splunk SOAR playbooks prioritize critical business functions for recovery, and the quarterly tabletop exercises incorporate risk management considerations, establishing post-incident operational norms.

mappings.control_title: Incident response plans and other risk management plans are evaluated and recommendations are communicated to leadership.
mappings.reasoning: The description states that recovery plans are tested quarterly with findings reviewed by the CISO and reported to leadership, fulfilling the evaluation and communication requirement.

mappings.control_title: Recovery actions are selected, scoped, prioritized, and performed.
mappings.reasoning: The automated playbooks in Splunk SOAR define specific recovery steps for various incident types, with prioritization based on critical applications, directly covering selection, scoping, prioritization, and performance of recovery actions.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-2025-003", "title": "Inadequate System Hardening, Input Validation, and Privacy Notice Compliance", "current_state": "The organization deploys software applications from standard repositories without verifying boot integrity using hardware-enforced non-modifiable storage. User input fields in web applications lack server-side validation, relying solely on client-side scripts. A general privacy policy exists on the corporate website but does not specifically address all PII processing activities or provide a clear notice to individuals. The system development life cycle (SDLC) is ad hoc, with no formal methodology enforced across projects. Position risk designations are not formally assigned to all roles, and the organization does not have a systematic process to receive or act upon security alerts and advisories from external sources.", "target_state": "The organization must load and execute the operating environment from hardware-enforced, non-modifiable storage (e.g., using signed boot loaders and measured boot) to prevent tampering. All information inputs must be validated for correctness, completeness, and authenticity on the server side. A privacy notice must be publicly available, clearly describing all PII processing, purposes, and sharing. The SDLC must follow a documented methodology with security checkpoints. Every organizational position must be assigned a risk designation (low, moderate, high) based on duties and access. The organization must establish a capability to receive, analyze, and respond to security alerts, advisories, and directives in a timely manner.", "gap_description": "The current state lacks hardware-enforced boot integrity, server-side input validation, a comprehensive privacy notice, a formal SDLC, position-specific risk designations, and a structured alert management process. These gaps expose the organization to risks of unauthorized code execution, data injection attacks, privacy violations, inconsistent system development, unmanaged insider threats, and delayed response to emerging vulnerabilities.", "recommended_actions": ["Enable Secure Boot and implement measured boot with hardware root of trust (e.g., TPM) for all endpoints and servers.", "Deploy server-side input validation libraries (e.g., OWASP ESAPI) and conduct dynamic application security testing (DAST) to cover all input vectors.", "Develop and publish a comprehensive privacy notice that includes categories of PII collected, purposes, third-party sharing, and individual rights, in plain language.", "Adopt a formal SDLC methodology (e.g., NIST SP 800-64) with mandatory security gates (requirements, design, coding, testing, deployment).", "Conduct a risk assessment of all positions and assign risk designations (low/moderate/high) based on access to sensitive systems or data.", "Subscribe to relevant threat intelligence feeds (e.g., US-CERT, vendor alerts) and implement a security advisory management process with defined triage and response timelines.", "Develop and enforce policies for position risk designation updates during role changes and periodic reviews."], "priority": "high", "estimated_effort": "months"}

mappings.control_title: Non-Modifiable Executable Programs
mappings.reasoning: The recommended action to enable Secure Boot and measured boot with TPM directly addresses SC-34's requirement to load and execute the operating environment from hardware-enforced, non-modifiable storage.

mappings.control_title: Information Input Validation
mappings.reasoning: The gap identifies missing server-side input validation, and the recommended action to deploy OWASP ESAPI and DAST directly maps to SI-10's requirement to check the validity of information inputs.

mappings.control_title: Privacy Notice
mappings.reasoning: The gap describes a generic privacy policy lacking specific PII processing details, and the recommended action to develop a comprehensive privacy notice aligns with PT-5's requirement for a publicly available notice describing PII processing activities.

mappings.control_title: System Development Life Cycle
mappings.reasoning: The ad hoc SDLC with no formal methodology is a gap, and the recommended action to adopt NIST SP 800-64 with security gates directly addresses SA-3's requirement to manage the system using a system development life cycle methodology.

mappings.control_title: Position Risk Designation
mappings.reasoning: The absence of formal position risk designations is identified as a gap, and the recommended action to assign risk designations based on access aligns with PS-2's requirement to assign a risk designation to all organizational positions.

mappings.control_title: Security Alerts, Advisories, and Directives
mappings.reasoning: The lack of a systematic process to receive security alerts is a gap, and the recommended action to subscribe to threat intelligence feeds and implement an advisory management process directly maps to SI-5's requirement to receive information system security alerts on an ongoing basis.

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Mobile Code and Cryptographic Boundary Protection Policy", "policy_text": "All mobile code, including scripts, applets, and active content, must be authorized, monitored, and controlled at all external and internal network boundaries. Cryptographic mechanisms used to protect the confidentiality of Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) during transmission across these boundaries shall employ FIPS 140-2 validated encryption. Mobile code execution logs must be reviewed weekly, and any unauthorized code shall be blocked and reported to the security team. Network boundary devices must enforce cryptographic controls and mobile code filtering to prevent unauthorized data exposure.", "department": "Information Security", "classification": "confidential"}

mappings.control_title: Control and monitor the use of mobile code.
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly requires authorization, monitoring, and control of mobile code at boundaries, including weekly log reviews and blocking unauthorized code.

mappings.control_title: Employ FIPS-validated cryptography when used to protect the confidentiality of CUI.
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates FIPS 140-2 validated encryption for protecting CUI confidentiality during transmission across boundaries.

mappings.control_title: Monitor, control, and protect organizational communications at the external boundaries and key internal boundaries.
mappings.reasoning: The policy directs monitoring and protection of communications at external and internal boundaries through cryptographic controls and mobile code filtering.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Insufficient Developer Configuration Management and Testing Documentation for Custom Procurement", "severity": "high", "description": "During the review of a custom-developed financial reporting module acquired from a third-party vendor, it was discovered that the organization did not enforce developer configuration management requirements per the acquisition contract. The vendor provided no evidence of version control processes, change logs, or a formal security testing and evaluation plan. Additionally, the organization lacks a documented policy and procedures for managing such acquisitions, resulting in inconsistent oversight.", "control_gaps": ["No formal developer configuration management plan was required or verified during procurement", "No security testing and evaluation plan was submitted or reviewed prior to deployment", "Absence of a documented system and services acquisition policy and procedures"], "remediation": "Develop and formally adopt a system and services acquisition policy that mandates configuration management and security testing requirements for all custom-developed components. Update procurement contracts to include specific references to developer configuration management (e.g., version control, change tracking) and require a security testing and evaluation plan prior to acceptance. Ensure all future acquisitions are reviewed against these documented procedures."}

mappings.control_title: Policy and Procedures
mappings.reasoning: The finding identifies a lack of documented acquisition policy and procedures, which is the core requirement of SA-1. The remediation explicitly calls for developing such a policy, indicating a direct gap in this control.

mappings.control_title: Developer Configuration Management
mappings.reasoning: The finding highlights that the vendor provided no version control or change log evidence, directly mapping to the requirement in SA-10 for developers to perform configuration management. The remediation emphasizes mandating such practices in contracts.

mappings.control_title: Developer Testing and Evaluation
mappings.reasoning: The finding notes the absence of a security testing and evaluation plan from the developer, which is the central requirement of SA-11. The remediation recommends requiring such a plan before acceptance, aligning with this control.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-SI-2023-001", "risk_statement": "An employee connects an unauthorized personal USB drive to a critical server, potentially introducing malware that could spread across the network and compromise sensitive data.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement automated endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to block unauthorized devices, enforce strict USB port controls via group policy, and conduct regular security awareness training on the risks of removable media."}

mappings.control_title: Provide protection from malicious code at designated locations within organizational systems.
mappings.reasoning: The risk involves potential introduction of malware via unauthorized USB device, which directly relates to the need for malicious code protection at entry points such as USB ports on critical servers.

mappings.control_title: Monitor organizational systems for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software.
mappings.reasoning: The scenario describes an unauthorized device (USB drive) being connected, which requires monitoring for unauthorized connections and devices to detect and prevent such actions.

mappings.control_title: Monitor organizational systems for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software.
mappings.reasoning: Reinforces the need for continuous monitoring to identify unauthorized software or devices that could introduce malicious code, as outlined in the risk of malware propagation.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-2024-001", "title": "Inadequate Data Encryption and Identity Verification for Remote Access", "current_state": "The organization currently uses a VPN for remote access but does not enforce full-disk encryption on employee laptops. User identities are verified only through username and password, without multi-factor authentication (MFA). Remote access logs are not regularly reviewed for anomalous activity.", "target_state": "All sensitive data at rest on mobile devices must be encrypted using FIPS 140-2 validated encryption. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) must be enforced for all remote access sessions. text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Authorized Software and System Capabilities Policy", "policy_text": "All systems within the organization must be configured to run only essential software and services necessary for their defined operational roles, with all non-essential capabilities disabled or removed. text: {"implementation_id": "impl-sc8-sa4-pl8-pt1-sa1-001", "control_area": "Transmission, Acquisition, and Architecture", "description": "The enterprise data center implements TLS 1.3 with AEAD ciphers (AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) for all inter-service and client-to-server communications, enforced via a centralized certificate authority (Hashicorp Vault) and automated with cert-manager in Kubernetes. This policy is reviewed annually by the CISO and Privacy Officer, and the architecture leverages network segmentation, encrypted tunnels (WireGuard for east-west traffic), and mutual TLS for authentication. The acquisition contracts include specific clauses requiring compliance with the enterprise\u2019s data transmission standards and privacy impact assessments for any PII handling. The overall approach ensures defense-in-depth by layering encryption, network segmentation, and policy enforcement.", "technologies": ["TLS 1.3", "AES-256-GCM", "ChaCha20-Poly1305", "Hashicorp Vault", "cert-manager", "Kubernetes", "WireGuard", "Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)", "Service Mesh (Istio)"], "configuration_details": "TLS 1.3 enforced with min_version=1.3 in Ingress and egress gateways; cipher suites restricted to TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 and TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256; mTLS enabled for all service-to-service communication in Istio with strict mode; Vault PKI engine issues certificates with 30-day validity; WireGuard tunnels configured with preshared keys and rotate every 24 hours; Acquisition contracts include clause 'Section 4.2 \u2013 Data Transmission Security' requiring adherence to the above; Architecture document (v2.3) maps network segments with encryption zones.", "evidence": "TLS configuration audit logs from Istio mesh telemetry; certificate issuance logs from Vault; network packet captures showing encrypted traffic; annual review minutes for PT-1 policy; signed acquisition contracts with security addenda; architecture diagram with encryption boundaries; compliance scanner reports (e.g., Qualys, Nessus) verifying TLS 1.3 enforcement.", "status": "implemented"}

text: {"implementation_id": "IMP-ENT-RESILIENCE-001", "control_area": "Enterprise Resilience and Incident Response", "description": "In a realistic enterprise environment, resilience and incident response are implemented through a combination of automated configuration management, redundant network architectures, and structured communication protocols. Configuration management is enforced using Ansible and Terraform with version-controlled Infrastructure as Code (IaC) templates, ensuring consistent deployment across environments. Network resilience is achieved via redundant MPLS and SD-WAN links with automatic failover, designed based on risk criticality for each business service. Incident notification is automated using Splunk and PagerDuty, which trigger alerts to internal stakeholders (e.g., SOC, IT management) and external stakeholders (e.g., customers, regulators) based on severity playbooks. Recovery processes include automated integrity checks using checksums and hash verification via HashiCorp Vault, followed by service restoration from encrypted backups in AWS S3 Glacier, with status confirmed through monitoring dashboards. Additionally, critical objectives and dependencies of external stakeholders are documented in a Business Impact Analysis (BIA) tool, such as ServiceNow, and communicated via quarterly reports and real-time status pages.", "technologies": ["Ansible", "Terraform", "Splunk", "PagerDuty", "HashiCorp Vault", "AWS S3 Glacier", "ServiceNow", "MPLS", "SD-WAN", "GitLab CI/CD"], "configuration_details": "Configuration management uses GitLab CI/CD pipelines with Ansible playbooks that enforce CIS benchmarks for all Linux servers. Network resilience configuration includes BGP with dual-homed MPLS circuits and SD-WAN with active-active load balancing; failover thresholds are set to 500ms. Incident notification routing in Splunk maps severity levels: critical incidents trigger SMS/email via PagerDuty to both internal SOC and external regulatory contacts within 15 minutes. Recovery integrity checks use SHA-256 hashes stored in Vault, compared post-restore; services are restored from daily snapshots in S3 Glacier with 1-hour RTO for critical assets. BIA data in ServiceNow includes RTO/RPO for each stakeholder service, reviewed quarterly.", "evidence": "Compliance is verified through automated audit logs from Ansible Tower showing configuration adherence; network failover test reports from quarterly drills; Splunk incident response reports with timestamps of notifications; Vault audit logs of hash verification steps; ServiceNow BIA reports with stakeholder sign-off; and restore test logs from AWS showing successful integrity checks.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: RS.CO-02 — Internal and external stakeholders are notified of incidents.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation describes automated notification via Splunk and PagerDuty to both internal (SOC, IT management) and external (customers, regulators) stakeholders based on severity, directly addressing RS.CO-02.

mappings.control_title: RC.RP-05 — The integrity of restored assets is verified, systems and services are restored, and normal operating status is confirmed.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation includes SHA-256 hash verification using HashiCorp Vault during recovery, restoration from encrypted backups, and status confirmation via monitoring dashboards, which aligns with RC.RP-05 requirements.

mappings.control_title: PR.PS-01 — Configuration management practices are established and applied.
mappings.reasoning: The use of Ansible and Terraform with version-controlled IaC templates in GitLab CI/CD pipelines, enforcing CIS benchmarks, demonstrates established configuration management practices per PR.PS-01.

mappings.control_title: PR.IR-02 — Network resilience and redundancy are designed commensurate with risk criticality.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation of redundant MPLS and SD-WAN links with automatic failover, designed based on risk criticality for each business service, directly satisfies PR.IR-02 for network resilience and redundancy.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-018", "title": "Inadequate Cryptographic Key Lifecycle Management and Third-Party Vendor Integration", "severity": "high", "description": "The organization uses a cloud-based document management system provided by a third-party vendor (DocSecure Inc.) to store and process sensitive data. text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-003", "risk_statement": "If the organization fails to maintain and test backups of critical data, a ransomware attack could encrypt production systems and result in permanent data loss, since recovery options would be unavailable. This scenario would also delay incident analysis and impede the selection of appropriate risk responses due to lack of tested recovery data.", "likelihood": "high", "impact": "very_high", "inherent_risk": "very_high", "residual_risk": "high", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement automated daily backups with encryption at rest and off-site storage, conduct quarterly restore tests, and integrate backup verification into incident response playbooks to ensure data availability and support timely risk response decisions."}

mappings.control_title: Risk responses are chosen from the available options, prioritized, planned, tracked, and communicated.

Without tested backups, the ability to choose effective responses and communicate recovery plans is compromised.

mappings.control_title: Potentially adverse events are analyzed to better understand associated activities.
mappings.reasoning: The risk highlights that a ransomware attack would require analysis of the event's scope and impact. Lack of tested backups hinders this analysis because recovery data may be incomplete or unreliable, limiting understanding of affected systems and data.

mappings.control_title: Backups of data are created, protected, maintained, and tested.
mappings.reasoning: The core risk directly addresses failure to maintain and test backups, which is the explicit focus of PR.DS-11. The scenario of permanent data loss from ransomware is a direct consequence of not meeting this control's requirements.

text: {"implementation_id": "impl-2025-03-21", "control_area": "Multiple NIST 800-53 and CMMC Controls", "description": "The implementation employs FIPS 140-2 validated cryptography for all data at rest and in transit, specifically using AES-256-GCM for storage encryption (e.g., BitLocker with TPM) and TLS 1.3 for network communications, governed by a centralized key management system (AWS KMS with automatic rotation). A dedicated cyber threat hunting team uses a combination of SIEM (Splunk) and EDR (CrowdStrike Falcon) to proactively search for indicators of compromise, with automated playbooks for incident tracking and reporting to CISA and internal IR teams. External system connections are strictly controlled via a next-gen firewall (Palo Alto) with application-layer filtering, and only approved VPNs (FIPS-validated) are allowed for remote access. Systems are hardened to least functionality using Group Policy Objects (GPOs) that disable unnecessary services (e.g., Windows Print Spooler, SMBv1) and enforce application whitelisting via AppLocker. Vulnerability remediation follows a risk-based priority queue, with critical vulnerabilities patched within 48 hours using automated patch management (Ivanti). Incidents are logged in a centralized ticketing system (ServiceNow) with mandatory fields for tracking and escalation to designated authorities.", "technologies": ["BitLocker (AES-256-GCM)", "AWS KMS", "TLS 1.3", "Splunk Enterprise Security", "CrowdStrike Falcon", "Palo Alto Networks NGFW", "Ivanti Patch Management", "ServiceNow IRM", "AppLocker", "Windows Group Policy"], "configuration_details": "BitLocker enabled on all endpoints with TPM protector and recovery key escrowed to Active Directory. TLS 1.3 enforced on all web servers with cipher suite TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384. Splunk configured with a threat hunting dashboard that queries EDR alerts for lateral movement patterns. Palo Alto firewall rules configured to deny all inbound traffic by default, with explicit allow rules for approved external systems (e.g., partner VPN endpoints). GPOs set to disable Windows features: Print Spooler (Startup=Disabled), SMBv1 (set SMB1Enabled to 0), PowerShell script execution restricted (ExecutionPolicy=RemoteSigned). Ivanti scans every 2 hours and auto-deploys critical patches within 48 hours. ServiceNow incident forms require CMMC fields: CUI involvement, severity, reporting authority (e.g., CISA, DoD CIO).", "evidence": "1) FIPS compliance: BitLocker recovery key reports from Active Directory, TLS scanner results showing only TLS 1.3. 2) Threat hunting: Splunk dashboards showing weekly hunt reports and CrowdStrike Falcon queries. 3) External connections: Palo Alto configuration backup showing deny-all default rule and approved tunnel logs. 4) Least functionality: GPO result reports from Microsoft Baseline Security Analyzer. 5) Vulnerability remediation: Ivanti patch compliance reports showing 98% patching within SLAs. 6) Incident tracking: ServiceNow reports with timestamps and designated authority notification logs.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Employ FIPS-validated cryptography when used to protect the confidentiality of CUI.
mappings.reasoning: The document describes use of FIPS 140-2 validated AES-256-GCM via BitLocker and TLS 1.3 with FIPS-compliant cipher suites, directly addressing the requirement for FIPS-validated cryptography to protect CUI confidentiality.

mappings.control_title: Establish and maintain a cyber threat hunting capability.
mappings.reasoning: The document explicitly states a dedicated threat hunting team using Splunk and CrowdStrike Falcon to proactively search for indicators of compromise, with automated dashboards and weekly hunt reports, fulfilling the requirement for an established threat hunting capability.

mappings.control_title: Verify and control/limit connections to and use of external systems.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation controls external system connections via a next-gen firewall with deny-all default, application-layer filtering, and approved FIPS-validated VPNs, directly verifying and limiting connections to external systems.

mappings.control_title: Track, document, and report incidents to designated officials and/or authorities.
mappings.reasoning: The document describes incident tracking in ServiceNow with mandatory fields for CUI involvement, severity, and reporting to designated authorities (CISA, DoD CIO), directly meeting the requirement for tracking, documenting, and reporting incidents.

mappings.control_title: Employ the principle of least functionality by configuring systems to provide only essential capabilities.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation uses GPOs to disable unnecessary services (Print Spooler, SMBv1) and enforces AppLocker application whitelisting, directly applying the principle of least functionality to reduce attack surface.

mappings.control_title: Remediate vulnerabilities in accordance with risk assessments.
mappings.reasoning: The document specifies a risk-based priority queue for vulnerability remediation with critical vulnerabilities patched within 48 hours via Ivanti automated patch management, aligning remediation with risk assessments.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-SEG-REC-001", "title": "Inadequate network segmentation and untested backup integrity for ransomware recovery", "current_state": "The organization has a flat network topology with no segmentation based on risk. Backups are performed daily but their integrity is never verified before restoration. Incident response plans for recovery are documented but have not been tested in over two years.", "target_state": "The organization should implement network segmentation to isolate critical assets and limit lateral movement of threats. Backup integrity must be verified through regular restoration tests before relying on them for recovery. The recovery plan should be exercised at least annually to ensure it can be executed effectively.", "gap_description": "The current flat network architecture and lack of backup integrity verification create a gap in identifying and responding to ransomware threats, as well as in recovering from such incidents with confidence. Without segmentation and tested recovery procedures, the organization cannot ensure timely restoration of operations or understand the full impact of adverse events.", "recommended_actions": ["Design and implement network segments based on risk classification (e.g., DMZ, internal, critical systems).", "Deploy firewalls and access controls between segments to restrict lateral movement.", "Establish a quarterly backup integrity verification process, including restoration tests in a sandbox environment.", "Conduct an annual tabletop exercise of the recovery portion of the incident response plan.", "Update the threat identification process to include ransomware-specific indicators.", "Integrate segmentation and backup verification results into the risk assessment and impact analysis workflows."], "priority": "critical", "estimated_effort": "months"}

mappings.control_title: ID.RA-03 — Internal and external threats to the organization are identified and recorded.
mappings.reasoning: The gap describes a flat network that hinders identification of internal threats like lateral movement, and untested backups that obscure external threats like ransomware. This directly impacts the ability to identify and record threats as required.

mappings.control_title: DE.AE-04 — The estimated impact and scope of adverse events are understood.
mappings.reasoning: Without network segmentation, the scope of a breach cannot be easily estimated because attackers can move freely. Untested backups obscure the impact of data loss. The gap prevents understanding the full impact and scope of adverse events.

mappings.control_title: PR.IR-01 — Networks and environments are segmented based on risk.
mappings.reasoning: The current state explicitly describes a flat network topology with no segmentation based on risk, which is a direct violation of PR.IR-01.

mappings.control_title: RC.RP-01 — The recovery portion of the incident response plan is executed once initiated from the incident response process or in response to a disaster.
mappings.reasoning: The gap mentions that the recovery plan is documented but untested. Without testing, the organization cannot ensure that the recovery portion can be executed effectively when needed, failing to meet RC.RP-01 requirements.

mappings.control_title: RC.RP-03 — The integrity of backups and other restoration assets is verified before using them for restoration.
mappings.reasoning: The current state explicitly states that backup integrity is never verified before restoration, which is a direct failure to meet RC.RP-03.

mappings.control_title: PR.PS-02 — Software is maintained and replaced in accordance with risk.
mappings.reasoning: The lack of network segmentation and untested backups increases the risk that outdated or vulnerable software on one segment can compromise the entire environment. This gap indicates that software maintenance and replacement are not aligned with the actual risk posture.

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Supplier Risk Management and Access Control Policy", "policy_text": "All suppliers with access to organizational systems or data must undergo a formal risk assessment prior to onboarding, with criticality tiers assigned based on data sensitivity and system interconnectivity. text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Unmanaged Contractor Access and Data Handling on External Systems", "severity": "high", "description": "Contractor maintenance personnel were granted administrative access to a cloud-based system hosting controlled unclassified information (CUI) without formal rules of behavior or authorization documentation. text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-016", "risk_statement": "Due to inconsistent time synchronization and lack of coordinated audit logging with external partners, forensic investigations cannot accurately reconstruct the sequence of events across organizational boundaries, potentially masking a coordinated cyber attack or data exfiltration.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement NTP-based time synchronization across all systems, enforce audit record generation for critical events (e.g., authentication, data access), and establish a cross-organizational audit logging agreement with external partners to define shared event categories, time source alignment, and log retention policies."}
