text: {"implementation_id": "impl-enterprise-001", "control_area": "Asset Prioritization and Risk Management", "description": "In this enterprise environment, assets are automatically classified and prioritized using a combination of ServiceNow CMDB for asset inventory and a custom risk scoring engine that evaluates criticality, business impact, and exposure. The risk scoring engine ingests threat intelligence feeds (e.g., Recorded Future, MITRE ATT&CK) and supply chain risk data from third-party vendor assessments (e.g., BitSight, Whistic) to dynamically adjust asset criticality. Secure software development practices are enforced via integrated SAST/DAST tools (e.g., Veracode, Burp Suite) in the CI/CD pipeline, with performance metrics tracked in a centralized dashboard. A standardized risk calculation method (using FAIR model) is documented and communicated through the GRC platform (e.g., RSA Archer), ensuring consistent evaluation across all business units.", "technologies": ["ServiceNow CMDB", "Recorded Future", "MITRE ATT&CK", "BitSight", "Whistic", "Veracode", "Burp Suite", "RSA Archer", "FAIR model"], "configuration_details": "Assets in ServiceNow are tagged with classification labels (e.g., 'critical', 'high', 'medium') based on business impact scores from quarterly BIA reviews. The risk scoring engine runs daily, combining CVSS scores from vulnerability scans, threat intelligence severity (e.g., Recorded Future risk score > 70), and supply chain vendor risk ratings (BitSight score < 600). The CI/CD pipeline enforces a policy: builds are blocked if Veracode findings exceed 'high' severity or Burp Suite identifies SQL injection. FAIR model calibrations are updated quarterly in RSA Archer and approved by risk committee.", "evidence": "Quarterly risk reports showing asset prioritization changes, dashboards with threat intelligence integration alerts, CI/CD pipeline logs with security gate violations, and RSA Archer audit trails of risk model updates.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: ID.AM-05 — Assets are prioritized based on classification, criticality, resources, and impact on the mission.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation describes asset classification via ServiceNow CMDB tags based on BIA and risk scores, directly addressing prioritization by criticality and impact.

mappings.control_title: DE.AE-07 — Cyber threat intelligence and other contextual information are integrated into the analysis.
mappings.reasoning: Threat intelligence feeds (Recorded Future, MITRE ATT&CK) are ingested into the risk scoring engine, integrating contextual threat data into asset analysis.

mappings.control_title: GV.RM — Risk Management Strategy — The organization's priorities, constraints, risk tolerance and appetite statements, and assumptions supporting operational risk decisions.

mappings.control_title: GV.SC-05 — Cybersecurity supply chain risk management planning is performed as part of the acquisition process.

mappings.control_title: PR.PS-06 — Secure software development practices are integrated and their performance is monitored throughout the software development life cycle.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-0037", "title": "Uncontrolled Use and Ownership of Portable Storage Media Containing CUI", "current_state": "The organization allows employees to use personal and unmarked USB drives, external hard drives, and other portable storage devices for transferring data, including CUI. mappings.control_title: Sensor Capability and Data

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-0042", "risk_statement": "An undetected credential compromise on a critical internal system could enable an attacker to exfiltrate sensitive data over an extended period, with the security operations center lacking the contextual intelligence to correlate the anomalous activities and failing to notify relevant stakeholders in a timely manner.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "very_high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement automated credential monitoring with behavioral analytics, integrate external threat intelligence feeds into the SIEM, establish a formal incident notification protocol with defined SLA requirements, and conduct quarterly tabletop exercises to validate analysis and response procedures."}

mappings.control_title: DE.AE-07 — Cyber threat intelligence and other contextual information are integrated into the analysis.

text: {"implementation_id": "MOB-ENC-001", "control_area": "Mobile and Wireless Security", "description": "The organization implements a comprehensive mobile device management (MDM) solution with integrated wireless access controls. mappings.reasoning: The implementation mandates full-disk encryption using FIPS 140-2 validated modules on all mobile devices managed by the MDM, directly fulfilling the requirement to encrypt CUI on mobile platforms.

mappings.control_title: Deny network communications traffic by default and allow network communications traffic by exception.
mappings.reasoning: The gap describes network switches with open ports and no default-deny rules, which contradicts SC.L2-3.13.6 requiring default deny and allow-by-exception for network traffic.

mappings.control_title: Update malicious code protection mechanisms when new releases are available.
mappings.reasoning: The gap highlights quarterly manual antivirus updates, failing to meet SI.L2-3.14.4 which mandates timely updates when new releases are available.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Unsecured IoT Sensor Fleet with Weak Key Management and Homogeneous Deployment", "severity": "high", "description": "The organization deploys a fleet of environmental IoT sensors (temperature, humidity, motion) across multiple facilities. These sensors can be remotely activated and configured via a cloud management portal, but the audit found that sensor-to-cloud communication uses hardcoded cryptographic keys shared across all devices. Additionally, all sensors are from a single vendor model, creating a monoculture that amplifies exploitation risk. The lack of per-device key management and the ability to remotely enable sensor capabilities without authorization violate security principles.", "control_gaps": ["Remote activation of sensor capabilities is not prohibited or logged, allowing unauthorized enabling of environmental sensing functions.", "Cryptographic keys for sensor communication are hardcoded and identical across all devices, with no key rotation or lifecycle management.", "The entire sensor fleet is homogeneous, increasing the blast radius if a single device vulnerability is discovered."], "remediation": "Implement a policy to prohibit and log remote activation of sensor capabilities, unless explicitly authorized for specific maintenance windows. Establish a per-device cryptographic key management process using a hardware security module (HSM) for key generation, distribution, and rotation. Diversify the sensor fleet by procuring devices from at least two different vendors with distinct firmware and hardware architectures."}

mappings.control_title: Sensor Capability and Data
mappings.reasoning: The finding describes remote activation of environmental sensors without prohibition or logging, directly violating SC-42's requirement to prohibit remote activation of sensing capabilities.

mappings.control_title: Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management
mappings.reasoning: The finding identifies hardcoded, shared cryptographic keys with no lifecycle management, which is a clear gap against SC-12's requirement for establishing and managing keys.

mappings.control_title: Heterogeneity
mappings.reasoning: The finding highlights a homogeneous sensor fleet from a single vendor, increasing exploitation risk, which maps to SC-29's call for employing diverse information technologies.

mappings.control_title: Protection of Information at Rest
mappings.reasoning: While the finding touches on key management for data in transit, it does not directly address encryption of stored sensor data, making this a weak mapping.

mappings.control_title: Least Privilege
mappings.reasoning: Remote activation without authorization implies a lack of access controls, but the finding focuses on sensor capabilities and keys rather than user privilege assignments, so this mapping is indirect.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-0042", "risk_statement": "Unauthorized configuration changes to critical network devices could bypass access controls, leading to undetected privilege escalation or data exfiltration, and incident analysis may be hampered by incomplete change logs.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement automated configuration change monitoring with real-time alerts, enforce change approval workflows with separation of duties, and integrate audit logs with the incident analysis platform to ensure complete visibility for forensics."}

mappings.control_title: Incident Analysis
mappings.reasoning: The risk highlights that incomplete change logs hamper incident analysis, directly relating to the need for investigations that support forensics and recovery.

mappings.control_title: Access Permissions and Authorizations
mappings.reasoning: The risk involves unauthorized changes bypassing access controls, which ties to the requirement for defined, managed, enforced permissions with least privilege and separation of duties.

mappings.control_title: Incident Report Triage and Validation
mappings.reasoning: The risk scenario implies that unauthorized changes may not be promptly triaged or validated as incidents, aligning with the need for triage and validation of incident reports.

mappings.control_title: Configuration Management Practices
mappings.reasoning: The risk explicitly addresses unauthorized configuration changes to critical devices, which directly maps to the requirement for established and applied configuration management practices.

mappings.control_title: Configuration Change Control
mappings.reasoning: The risk focuses on unauthorized changes bypassing controls, which directly maps to the need for implemented configuration change control to manage and approve changes.

text: {"implementation_id": "imp-session-isolation-001", "control_area": "System and Communications Protection", "description": "Session authenticity is enforced through mutual TLS (mTLS) for all inter-service communications, ensuring both endpoints are verified. Transmission confidentiality and integrity are achieved using TLS 1.3 with AEAD ciphers (e.g., AES-256-GCM) across all network paths. Process isolation is implemented via containerization with Kubernetes pods running under distinct Linux namespaces (PID, network, mount) and seccomp profiles, preventing cross-process interference. Configuration details include setting the TLS cipher suite to TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, enabling mTLS with certificate rotation every 24 hours, and applying a default-deny network policy in Kubernetes. Evidence includes automated TLS handshake logs, network policy audit reports, and container runtime seccomp logs from Falco.", "technologies": ["Kubernetes", "Envoy Proxy", "Istio Service Mesh", "Falco", "Cert-Manager", "HashiCorp Vault"], "configuration_details": "Istio mesh configured with STRICT mTLS mode for all services; TLS cipher suite set to TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384; Kubernetes PodSecurityPolicy with runAsNonRoot: true and seccomp profile 'runtime/default'; network policies deny all ingress/egress except for allowed ports; Vault issues short-lived certificates with 24h TTL; Falco rules monitor for seccomp violations and namespace escape attempts.", "evidence": "TLS handshake logs from Envoy showing mTLS (peer certificates verified); Kubernetes audit logs for network policy enforcement; Falco alerts for seccomp violations; Vault certificate issuance logs; periodic penetration test reports confirming session authenticity and isolation.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Session Authenticity
mappings.reasoning: The implementation uses mTLS with short-lived certificates via Istio and Vault to verify both client and server identities for every session, directly protecting session authenticity as required by SC-23.

mappings.control_title: Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity
mappings.reasoning: TLS 1.3 with AES-256-GCM is configured for all inter-service communications, ensuring confidentiality and integrity of transmitted data in transit, which maps to SC-8.

mappings.control_title: Process Isolation
mappings.reasoning: Kubernetes containers run under separate Linux namespaces (PID, network, mount) with seccomp profiles and PodSecurityPolicies enforcing non-root execution, providing strong process isolation as required by SC-39.

mappings.control_title: Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management
mappings.reasoning: The use of HashiCorp Vault for issuing short-lived TLS certificates and managing keys aligns with SC-12, as it establishes and manages cryptographic keys for session authentication and transmission protection.

mappings.control_title: Boundary Protection
mappings.reasoning: Kubernetes network policies that deny all traffic except explicitly allowed ports, combined with Istio's mTLS enforcement at the service mesh boundary, implement boundary protection controls per SC-7.

mappings.control_title: Content of Audit Records
mappings.reasoning: The evidence collection from TLS handshake logs, Vault certificate issuance logs, and Falco seccomp alerts provides detailed audit records that include session details, key management events, and process isolation checks, supporting AU-3 requirements for audit record content.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-RS-MA-001", "title": "Lack of Formal Incident Recovery Initiation Criteria and Escalation Procedures", "current_state": "The organization currently relies on ad-hoc judgment by the incident response team to determine when recovery actions should begin after an incident. Incidents are categorized using a basic priority matrix, but there are no documented criteria for escalating incidents to higher management or external parties. The current process lacks formal definitions for recovery triggers, prioritization levels, and escalation thresholds.", "target_state": "The organization should have documented, agreed-upon criteria for initiating incident recovery, including specific conditions and decision points. Incidents must be consistently categorized and prioritized using a defined scheme that accounts for impact and urgency. A formal escalation process should be in place to elevate incidents to appropriate stakeholders, including management and external entities, based on severity and complexity.", "gap_description": "The absence of formal recovery initiation criteria, inconsistent incident categorization and prioritization, and undefined escalation procedures create a significant compliance gap against NIST CSF controls RS.MA-05, RS.MA-03, and RS.MA-04.", "recommended_actions": ["Develop and document incident recovery initiation criteria, including severity thresholds and decision-making authority.", "Define and implement a standardized incident categorization and prioritization framework (e.g., based on impact, data sensitivity, and business criticality).", "Establish clear escalation procedures, including triggers, communication channels, and roles/responsibilities for escalation to management and external partners.", "Conduct tabletop exercises to validate the new recovery initiation, prioritization, and escalation processes.", "Update incident response plans and playbooks to reflect the new criteria and procedures."], "priority": "high", "estimated_effort": "weeks"}

mappings.control_title: Incident recovery initiation criteria are defined and agreed to.
mappings.reasoning: The gap entry describes the lack of formal, documented criteria for initiating incident recovery, which directly maps to RS.MA-05's requirement that recovery initiation criteria be defined and agreed upon.

mappings.control_title: Incidents are categorized and prioritized.
mappings.reasoning: The gap entry highlights inconsistent incident categorization and prioritization, which maps to RS.MA-03's requirement for incidents to be categorized and prioritized to guide response actions.

mappings.control_title: Incidents are escalated or elevated as needed.
mappings.reasoning: The gap entry identifies undefined escalation procedures, which maps to RS.MA-04's requirement that incidents be escalated or elevated as needed based on severity and organizational protocols.

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Remote Access and Audit Protection Policy", "policy_text": "All personnel accessing Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) from alternate work sites must use approved mobile computing platforms with full-disk encryption enabled. Access to CUI and audit logs shall be granted on a least-privilege basis, with privileged accounts restricted to authorized administrators. Audit logs and logging tools must be stored in access-controlled repositories and protected against unauthorized modification or deletion. Remote work sites must implement physical and logical safeguards, including locked storage and encrypted connections, to prevent unauthorized access to CUI.", "department": "Information Security", "classification": "confidential"}

mappings.control_title: Employ the principle of least privilege, including for specific security functions and privileged accounts.
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly states that access to CUI and audit logs shall be granted on a least-privilege basis, with privileged accounts restricted to authorized administrators, directly addressing the least privilege requirement.

mappings.control_title: Encrypt CUI on mobile devices and mobile computing platforms.
mappings.reasoning: The policy requires approved mobile computing platforms to have full-disk encryption enabled, which aligns with encrypting CUI on mobile devices and platforms.

mappings.control_title: Protect audit information and audit logging tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion.
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates that audit logs and logging tools be stored in access-controlled repositories and protected against unauthorized modification or deletion, directly mapping to this audit protection control.

mappings.control_title: Enforce safeguarding measures for CUI at alternate work sites.
mappings.reasoning: The policy addresses personnel accessing CUI from alternate work sites and requires physical and logical safeguards such as locked storage and encrypted connections, which enforces safeguarding measures for CUI at alternate work sites.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-042", "risk_statement": "Inadequate physical security and environmental monitoring at the primary data center could allow unauthorized individuals to gain access to sensitive systems or cause equipment damage due to uncontrolled temperature and humidity, resulting in data breaches or extended service outages.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement and enforce a formal physical and environmental protection policy, install environmental sensors with automated alerts for temperature and humidity, and maintain detailed visitor access logs with periodic audits."}

mappings.control_title: Policy and Procedures
mappings.reasoning: The risk statement implies a lack of documented policies for physical and environmental protection. Mitigation requires developing, documenting, and disseminating such policies, which directly maps to PE-1.

mappings.control_title: Visitor Access Records
mappings.reasoning: The risk of unauthorized access is addressed by maintaining visitor access records. The treatment description explicitly calls for detailed logs and audits, aligning with PE-8.

mappings.control_title: Environmental Controls
mappings.reasoning: The risk mentions equipment damage from uncontrolled temperature/humidity. The treatment includes environmental sensors and alerts to maintain acceptable limits, which corresponds to PE-14.

text: {"implementation_id": "impl-remote-maint-encrypt-001", "control_area": "Access Control and Maintenance", "description": "The organization employs a Jump Server with both IPsec VPN (IKEv2) and SSH tunneling to protect the confidentiality of all remote access and nonlocal maintenance sessions. All remote administrative connections must pass through the Jump Server, which enforces the principle of least privilege by restricting each administrator to only the systems and commands necessary for their role. Multifactor authentication (MFA) via RSA SecurID tokens is required before any nonlocal maintenance session is established over external networks. Additionally, all stored logs, configuration backups, and CUI data on the Jump Server are encrypted at rest using AES-256 (BitLocker) to ensure confidentiality.", "technologies": ["Cisco ASA 5500 series (IPsec VPN)", "OpenSSH 8.9 (for SSH tunneling and bastion host)", "RSA SecurID (MFA tokens)", "Microsoft BitLocker (AES-256)", "Active Directory Group Policy (least privilege RBAC)"], "configuration_details": "IPsec IKEv2 configured with AES-256-GCM encryption and SHA-384 hashing; SSH server on Jump Server set to PermitRootLogin no, AllowGroups 'admins'; MFA required via PAM module for SSH; BitLocker enabled on all Jump Server drives with TPM+PIN protector; AD groups: 'JumpAdmins' (full access), 'JumpOperators' (limited commands via sudoers).", "evidence": "Screenshots of VPN configuration showing AES-256; SSH server logs showing MFA challenge; BitLocker recovery key backup audit; AD group membership reports; sudoers file showing command restrictions.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: AC.L2-3.1.13 — Employ cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of remote access sessions.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation specifies IPsec VPN with AES-256-GCM encryption to protect all remote access sessions, directly addressing the requirement to employ cryptographic mechanisms for confidentiality.

mappings.control_title: MA.L2-3.7.5 — Require multifactor authentication to establish nonlocal maintenance sessions via external network connections.
mappings.reasoning: The description explicitly requires RSA SecurID MFA for all nonlocal maintenance sessions through the Jump Server, satisfying the multifactor authentication requirement for external network connections.

mappings.control_title: AC.L2-3.1.5 — Employ the principle of least privilege, including for specific security functions and privileged accounts.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation enforces least privilege through Active Directory groups (JumpAdmins vs. JumpOperators) and restricted sudoers commands, limiting each administrator to only necessary systems and functions.

mappings.control_title: SC.L2-3.13.16 — Protect the confidentiality of CUI at rest.
mappings.reasoning: The description states that all stored logs, configuration backups, and CUI data on the Jump Server are encrypted at rest using AES-256 BitLocker, directly protecting CUI confidentiality at rest.

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Third-Party and External Stakeholder Dependency Management Policy", "policy_text": "The organization shall identify, document, and communicate all critical services, capabilities, and objectives that external stakeholders depend on from the organization, as well as those the organization depends on from third parties. All legal, regulatory, and contractual cybersecurity obligations, including privacy and civil liberties requirements, must be mapped to these dependencies and reviewed annually. The Chief Security Officer shall maintain a registry of dependencies and associated compliance responsibilities, which shall be updated within 30 days of any material change. Failure to communicate dependency changes to affected stakeholders may result in corrective action as per the organization's governance framework.", "department": "Office of the Chief Security Officer", "classification": "internal"}

mappings.control_title: Critical objectives, capabilities, and services that external stakeholders depend on or expect from the organization are understood and communicated.
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly requires identification, documentation, and communication of critical services and capabilities that external stakeholders depend on from the organization, directly aligning with GV.OC-04.

mappings.control_title: Outcomes, capabilities, and services that the organization depends on from others are understood and communicated.
mappings.reasoning: The policy states the organization shall identify dependencies on third parties for services and capabilities, and communicate changes to stakeholders, mapping directly to GV.OC-05.

mappings.control_title: Legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements regarding cybersecurity — including privacy and civil liberties obligations — are understood and managed.
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates mapping all legal, regulatory, and contractual cybersecurity obligations (including privacy and civil liberties) to dependencies and reviewing them annually, which aligns with GV.OC-03.

mappings.control_title: Critical objectives, capabilities, and services that external stakeholders depend on or expect from the organization are understood and communicated.
mappings.reasoning: The policy specifies a registry of dependencies with compliance responsibilities and a 30-day update requirement for material changes, further supporting the communication and understanding emphasis of GV.OC-04.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-012", "title": "Uncontrolled User-Installed Software with Inconsistent Configuration Baselines", "severity": "high", "description": "During the audit, it was discovered that users on multiple workstations have the ability to install software without administrative approval or oversight. Additionally, the security configuration settings for these systems are not consistently enforced, leading to deviations from the approved baseline. This combination of unmonitored software installation and non-standard configurations creates significant risk for unauthorized changes and potential exploitation.", "control_gaps": ["Lack of application whitelisting or blacklisting mechanisms to control user-installed software", "No enforced security configuration baselines for workstations, allowing drift from secure settings", "Absence of documented approval process for software installations or configuration changes"], "remediation": "Implement a centralized endpoint management solution that enforces application whitelisting and restricts installation privileges to authorized personnel. Establish and automatically deploy hardened security configuration baselines for all workstations, and enforce them via group policy or similar mechanisms. Require a formal change request and approval process for any software installation or configuration deviation."}

mappings.control_title: Control and monitor user-installed software.
mappings.reasoning: The finding directly describes users installing software without oversight, which is the core gap addressed by CM.L2-3.4.9. The lack of whitelisting or monitoring mechanisms is a clear failure to control and monitor user-installed software.

mappings.control_title: Establish and enforce security configuration settings for information technology products employed in organizational systems.
mappings.reasoning: The finding identifies that security configuration settings are not consistently enforced, leading to deviations from the approved baseline. This is a direct violation of establishing and enforcing security configuration settings as required by CM.L2-3.4.2.

mappings.control_title: Define, document, approve, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes to organizational systems.
mappings.reasoning: The finding highlights a lack of documented approval process for software installations or configuration changes. This maps to the requirement to define, document, and approve access restrictions for changes, as the absence of approval processes undermines control over logical access changes.

mappings.control_title: Least Functionality
mappings.reasoning: The finding's emphasis on uncontrolled software installation relates to the principle of least functionality (CM-7), which requires organizations to configure systems to provide only essential capabilities and prohibit or restrict the use of unnecessary functions. The ability to install arbitrary software violates this principle.

mappings.control_title: Baseline Configuration
mappings.reasoning: The finding notes that security configuration settings are not consistently enforced, leading to deviations from the approved baseline. This directly maps to CM-2, which requires organizations to develop, document, and maintain baseline configurations for information systems.

mappings.control_title: Configuration Change Control
mappings.reasoning: The absence of a documented approval process for software installations or configuration changes maps to CM-3, which requires organizations to document, review, approve, and track changes to systems. The finding indicates a failure in change control processes.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-0042", "risk_statement": "Lack of diversity in cloud service providers and encryption protocols may allow an attacker to exploit a single vendor vulnerability to compromise both data in transit and data at rest across the organization's multi-region deployment.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement a multi-vendor cloud strategy by integrating at least two independent cloud providers, enforce mandatory TLS 1.3 for all data in transit, and deploy AES-256 encryption with customer-managed keys for all data at rest. Conduct quarterly vendor diversity reviews and encryption audits."}

mappings.control_title: Heterogeneity
mappings.reasoning: The risk specifically addresses a lack of technology diversity (single cloud provider) which could lead to a single point of exploitation. The treatment recommends multi-vendor cloud strategy, directly aligning with SC-29's requirement to employ a diverse set of information technologies to limit exploitation risk.

mappings.control_title: Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity
mappings.reasoning: The risk statement mentions compromise of 'data in transit' and the treatment explicitly mandates TLS 1.3 for all transmitted data. This maps directly to SC-8 which requires protecting the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information.

mappings.control_title: Protection of Information at Rest
mappings.reasoning: The risk includes compromise of 'data at rest' and the treatment prescribes AES-256 encryption with customer-managed keys. This aligns with SC-28's requirement to protect the confidentiality and integrity of information at rest.

mappings.control_title: Heterogeneity
mappings.reasoning: The risk also touches on encryption protocol diversity (single protocol reliance). By enforcing TLS 1.3 and AES-256, the organization introduces cryptographic diversity, further supporting SC-29's goal of reducing common-mode failures through heterogeneity.

text: {"implementation_id": "IMP-CMMC-CAM-SEC-001", "control_area": "Collaborative Computing Device Security, Session Management, Authentication Feedback", "description": "In our enterprise environment, collaborative computing devices (webcams, microphones, and screen-sharing software) are managed via Group Policy and endpoint security agents to disable remote activation. A physical LED indicator is required for all webcams, and a system tray icon with a tooltip shows when a microphone or camera is in use. Session lock is enforced through Windows Group Policy, setting a 15-minute inactivity timeout that triggers a lock screen with a pattern-hiding display (blank screen with no data preview). Authentication feedback is obscured by configuring Windows to display only asterisks during password entry and disabling password reveal buttons via registry settings, while also ensuring that single sign-on prompts do not echo the username after failed attempts.", "technologies": ["Microsoft Windows 10/11 Enterprise", "Active Directory Group Policy", "Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager", "CrowdStrike Falcon endpoint detection and response", "Zoom and Microsoft Teams with admin-controlled settings", "BitLocker Drive Encryption", "Windows Hello for Business"], "configuration_details": "Group Policy Object 'CAM-SEC-POLICY': Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Remote Desktop Services > Remote Desktop Session Host > Devices and Resource Redirection: 'Do not allow audio capture redirection' enabled. Local Computer Policy > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options: 'Interactive logon: Do not display last user name' enabled. User Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > Power Management > Video and Display Settings: 'Turn off the display' set to 5 minutes, 'Require a password on wakeup' enabled. Registry key HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows\\Personalization: 'NoLockScreenCamera' set to 1. Zoom admin settings: 'Disable remote control of camera' enforced. Teams admin center: 'Require physical indicator for camera' enabled. Windows Hello PIN complexity enforced via Group Policy.", "evidence": "1) Screenshot of Group Policy Management Editor showing the 'Do not allow audio capture redirection' setting. 2) Resultant Set of Policy (RSOP) report for a test workstation confirming the 15-minute screen saver timeout and password-on-wake settings. 3) Active Directory audit log showing the GPO linked to all workstations in the 'CMMC-COMPLIANT' OU. 4) Zoom admin dashboard showing 'Disable remote camera activation' enabled for all users. 5) Registry export from a sample workstation showing the 'NoLockScreenCamera' value set to 1. 6) Security baseline scan report from Microsoft Security Compliance Toolkit confirming obscure feedback settings.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Prohibit remote activation of collaborative computing devices and provide indication of devices in use to users present at the device.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation explicitly disables remote activation of webcams and microphones via Group Policy and admin settings for Zoom/Teams, and requires physical LED indicators and system tray notifications to show when devices are in use, directly satisfying the control's requirements.

mappings.control_title: Use session lock with pattern-hiding displays to prevent access and viewing of data after a period of inactivity.
mappings.reasoning: The 15-minute inactivity timeout, password-on-wake requirement, and blank screen (pattern-hiding display) configuration via Group Policy meet the session lock and data-viewing prevention requirements of this control.

mappings.control_title: Obscure feedback of authentication information.
mappings.reasoning: The use of asterisks for password fields, disabling of password reveal buttons, and hiding the last logged-on username via Group Policy obscure authentication feedback, aligning with the control's intent to prevent shoulder surfing and credential exposure.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-014", "title": "Inconsistent Personnel Security and Privacy Practices for External Contractors Handling Incident Response Data", "severity": "high", "description": "External contractors with access to incident response systems and sensitive privacy data are not required to re-authenticate after idle sessions, and the organization lacks documented procedures ensuring these contractors meet internal personnel security standards. Additionally, a covert channel analysis has not been performed on the incident response platform, potentially allowing information leakage of privacy program plan details. The incident response policy does not address the unique risks posed by external personnel, and the privacy program plan fails to incorporate contractor oversight.", "control_gaps": ["External personnel security requirements are not enforced or documented for contractors accessing incident response systems.", "Privacy program plan does not include provisions for external personnel handling privacy data.", "No covert channel analysis has been conducted on incident response tools used by external contractors.", "Incident response policy lacks procedures for verifying contractor compliance with personnel security.", "Re-authentication is not enforced for external contractors after periods of inactivity on sensitive systems."], "remediation": "Update the incident response policy and privacy program plan to explicitly include external personnel security requirements, including re-authentication after idle periods. Perform covert channel analysis on all incident response platforms accessible by external contractors. Ensure all contractors undergo the same personnel security vetting as internal staff and document these procedures."}

mappings.control_title: External Personnel Security
mappings.reasoning: The finding explicitly states that external contractors do not meet the same personnel security requirements as internal staff, directly addressing PS-7's requirement to ensure external providers meet organizational personnel security standards.

mappings.control_title: Privacy Program Plan
mappings.reasoning: The finding notes the privacy program plan fails to include contractor oversight, which is a core element of a comprehensive privacy program plan under PM-19.

mappings.control_title: Covert Channel Analysis
mappings.reasoning: The finding identifies that no covert channel analysis has been performed on the incident response platform, which could allow information leakage, directly mapping to SC-31's requirement to analyze for covert channels.

mappings.control_title: Policy and Procedures
mappings.reasoning: The incident response policy lacks procedures for verifying contractor compliance with personnel security, indicating a gap in documented policy and procedures as required by IR-1.

mappings.control_title: Re-Authentication
mappings.reasoning: The finding states that re-authentication is not enforced for external contractors after idle sessions, which is a direct violation of IA-11's requirement to re-authenticate users under defined circumstances.

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-0042", "risk_statement": "The organization may process personally identifiable information (PII) without a documented legal basis or clear notice to data subjects, leading to potential regulatory fines, loss of customer trust, and legal action from data protection authorities.", "likelihood": "high", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement a comprehensive privacy governance program including a publicly posted privacy notice, documented authority for each PII processing activity, and a PII processing purposes registry. mappings.control_title: Privacy Notice

mappings.control_title: Authority to Process PII

mappings.control_title: PII Processing Purposes

mappings.control_title: Dissemination of Privacy Program Information

mappings.control_title: Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning
mappings.reasoning: The annual review and update of privacy notices as part of treatment requires periodic assessment, which loosely maps to RA-5's monitoring and scanning concept applied to privacy controls.

text: {"implementation_id": "impl-enterprise-001", "control_area": "Identification and Authentication, Fail-Safe, Planning, Emergency Power, Testing", "description": "The enterprise implements a centralized identity and access management (IAM) system with multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all user access. Fail-safe procedures are enforced via redundant power systems and automated shutdown scripts that preserve system state during failures. A quarterly Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) is maintained in a governance tool, tracking security deficiencies and remediation. Emergency power is provided by UPS units in all data centers, configured for 30-minute runtime to ensure orderly shutdown. Developer testing follows a standard security testing plan (ST&E) that includes static code analysis and penetration testing for all critical applications.", "technologies": ["Okta Identity Cloud", "Palo Alto Networks Firewalls", "APC Smart-UPS 3000", "Jira (for POA&M tracking)", "SonarQube (static analysis)", "Burp Suite (penetration testing)"], "configuration_details": "MFA enforced for all users with adaptive policies based on location and device. UPS units configured for automatic shutdown via PowerChute Network Shutdown at 5% battery remaining. POA&M updated quarterly with 30-day SLA for critical findings. Developer ST&E plan includes 95% code coverage for security-critical modules.", "evidence": "Audit logs show MFA enforcement across all user sessions. UPS event logs confirm automatic shutdown sequences in last 3 power outages. POA&M reports signed by CISO and available on shared drive. Developer test results archived in CI/CD pipeline with pass/fail status.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Policy and Procedures
mappings.reasoning: The document describes an IAM system with MFA, which is a procedural implementation of identification and authentication policy. The use of Okta and adaptive policies reflects documented procedures for user authentication.

mappings.control_title: Fail-Safe Procedures
mappings.reasoning: The automated shutdown scripts and redundant power systems ensure the system fails securely, preserving state during failures. This directly implements SI-17 fail-safe procedures.

mappings.control_title: Plan of Action and Milestones
mappings.reasoning: The quarterly POA&M in Jira with SLAs for remediation directly addresses CA-5 requirements for developing and updating a plan of action and milestones.

mappings.control_title: Emergency Power
mappings.reasoning: APC UPS units configured for 30-minute runtime and automatic shutdown provide short-term emergency power, mapping to PE-11 requirements.

mappings.control_title: Developer Testing and Evaluation
mappings.reasoning: The developer ST&E plan with SonarQube and Burp Suite, including code coverage targets, demonstrates a security testing and evaluation plan as required by SA-11.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-IR-001", "title": "Incomplete Incident Handling Capability and Reporting Process", "current_state": "The organization has a basic, ad-hoc incident response process that relies on informal communication among IT staff. text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Data-at-Rest Encryption and Physical Security Policy", "policy_text": "All sensitive data stored on servers, workstations, or portable media must be encrypted using FIPS 140-2 validated algorithms. Physical access to server rooms and data centers must be restricted to authorized personnel only, with access logs reviewed weekly. Software development teams must follow secure coding practices and undergo quarterly security training, with code reviews conducted prior to deployment. Environmental monitoring systems, including surveillance cameras and motion detectors, must be operational 24/7 to detect unauthorized physical access or adverse events. The Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) shall oversee the implementation of this policy and report compliance metrics to the executive risk management committee on a monthly basis.", "department": "Information Security", "classification": "confidential"}

mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates secure coding practices, quarterly security training, and pre-deployment code reviews, integrating and monitoring secure practices across the software development lifecycle.

mappings.control_title: The physical environment is monitored to find potentially adverse events.

mappings.control_title: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-use are protected.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Inadequate Media Sanitization and Risk Management Oversight for Disposal of Critical Infrastructure Systems Containing PII", "severity": "high", "description": "During a review of media disposal procedures for systems supporting critical infrastructure, it was discovered that hard drives and other storage media are not consistently sanitized before being decommissioned or sent for recycling. text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Critical System Availability and Resilience Policy", "policy_text": "All critical systems must maintain a minimum availability level of 99.99% (four nines) measured monthly, with documented capacity planning reviews performed quarterly. text: {"implementation_id": "IMP-CROSS-DOMAIN-SESSION-001", "control_area": "Session Authenticity and Cross-Domain Security", "description": "In a multi-domain enterprise environment, TLS 1.3 is enforced for all inter-domain communications to ensure session authenticity and prevent man-in-the-middle attacks. DNSSEC is deployed on all authoritative DNS servers to provide data origin authentication for name resolution, using NSEC3 for denial of existence. A cross-domain data transfer appliance (CDTA) with a hardware security module (HSM) enforces policy by requiring cryptographic signing of all files transferred between security domains, with automated validation at the receiving end. The system uses mutual TLS (mTLS) with client certificates issued by an internal certificate authority (CA) to authenticate both ends of each session.", "technologies": ["TLS 1.3", "DNSSEC with NSEC3", "BIND 9.16", "Cross-domain data transfer appliance (CDTA)", "Hardware Security Module (HSM) - Thales Luna SA", "Internal Certificate Authority (CA) - Microsoft AD CS", "mutual TLS (mTLS)"], "configuration_details": "TLS 1.3 enforced with cipher suite TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384; certificate pinning enabled. DNSSEC configured with algorithm ECDSA P-256 and NSEC3 iterations set to 10. Cross-domain policy enforced via CDTA whitelist of allowed file types (e.g., .pdf, .xlsx) and mandatory signing with SHA-256 hash; all transfers logged to SIEM. mTLS requires client certificates with key usage 'digitalSignature' and 'keyEncipherment', validity 1 year, issued by internal CA.", "evidence": "TLS configuration captured via SSL Labs server test; DNSSEC validation logs from BIND showing RRSIG records; CDTA audit logs showing signed file transfers with cryptographic hash verification; SIEM alerts on policy violations; quarterly certificate revocation list (CRL) review.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Session Authenticity
mappings.reasoning: The implementation enforces TLS 1.3 with mutual TLS and certificate pinning, which protects the authenticity of communications sessions by ensuring both endpoints are authenticated and session data is integrity-protected against hijacking.

mappings.control_title: Secure Name/Address Resolution Service (Authoritative Source)
mappings.reasoning: DNSSEC with NSEC3 is deployed on authoritative DNS servers using BIND 9.16 and ECDSA P-256 signatures, providing data origin authentication and integrity verification for name resolution responses.

mappings.control_title: Cross-Domain Policy Enforcement
mappings.reasoning: The cross-domain data transfer appliance (CDTA) with HSM enforces policy on all data transferred between security domains by requiring cryptographic signing, file type whitelisting, and logging to SIEM, ensuring only authorized and integrity-verified data crosses domains.

text: {"gap_id": "CM-GAP-005", "title": "Lack of Formal Change Management and Configuration Control Processes", "current_state": "The organization currently relies on ad-hoc change management practices where system administrators make changes directly to production systems without formal documentation or approval. text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Incident Recovery and Data Protection Policy", "policy_text": "All organizational systems must have a documented incident recovery plan that is tested quarterly, with restoration activities executed within four hours of incident declaration to ensure operational availability. Recovery procedures must include secure restoration of data-at-rest using encryption and integrity verification to protect confidentiality, integrity, and availability. During incidents, software and system components must be assessed for risk and replaced or patched as necessary to prevent expansion of the event and mitigate effects. Identities and credentials used in recovery operations must be verified and managed through the organization\u2019s identity management system, with access logged and reviewed. Incident-related information, including restoration status and mitigation actions, must be shared with designated internal stakeholders and external partners in accordance with pre-established sharing agreements. All recovery and mitigation activities must be documented and reported to the compliance department within 24 hours.", "department": "Information Security and Compliance", "classification": "confidential"}

mappings.control_title: RS.CO-03
mappings.reasoning: The policy requires sharing incident-related information with designated internal and external stakeholders per pre-established agreements, which maps to RS.CO-03's focus on information sharing consistent with response plans and sharing agreements.

mappings.control_title: PR.PS-02
mappings.reasoning: The policy states that software and system components must be assessed for risk and replaced or patched as necessary during incidents, which aligns with PR.PS-02's requirement to maintain and replace software in accordance with risk.

mappings.control_title: Incident Mitigation
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly requires activities to prevent expansion of the event and mitigate effects through risk assessment, patching, and replacement of components, directly mapping to RS.MI's goal of preventing expansion and mitigating incident effects.

mappings.control_title: PR.DS-01
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates secure restoration of data-at-rest using encryption and integrity verification to protect confidentiality, integrity, and availability, which directly maps to PR.DS-01's requirement to protect data-at-rest.

mappings.control_title: PR.AA-01
mappings.reasoning: The policy requires that identities and credentials used in recovery operations be verified and managed through the organization's identity management system with logged access reviews, aligning with PR.AA-01's mandate to manage identities and credentials for authorized users, services, and hardware.

text: {"implementation_id": "IMP-CM-RA-MP-001", "control_area": "Configuration Management, Risk Assessment, Media Protection", "description": "In our enterprise environment, we implement least functionality by deploying a standardized server image that disables all unnecessary services, ports, and protocols via Group Policy and Ansible automation. Vulnerability remediation is performed weekly using Tenable.io scans; critical and high-severity findings are patched within 48 hours and medium within 14 days, with all remediations tracked in ServiceNow. For media protection, all CUI stored on removable media (USB drives, external hard drives) is encrypted using BitLocker To Go with AES-256, and transport is logged and approved through a ticketing system.", "technologies": ["Windows Server 2022", "Group Policy Management Console", "Ansible Tower", "Tenable.io", "ServiceNow", "BitLocker To Go", "Active Directory"], "configuration_details": "Group Policy: Configured to disable PowerShell v2, SMBv1, Telnet Client, and all non-essential Windows Features. Ansible playbooks enforce that only necessary services (e.g., DNS, DHCP, IIS) are enabled per role. Tenable.io scans are scheduled weekly with custom policies for CUI systems, and vulnerability SLAs are enforced. BitLocker To Go is enforced via Group Policy requiring password or smart card authentication for removable media, with recovery keys stored in Active Directory.", "evidence": "Evidence includes: (1) Group Policy reports showing disabled features; (2) Ansible Tower job logs; (3) Tenable.io scan results and remediation tickets; (4) ServiceNow change requests for patching; (5) BitLocker recovery key escrow records; (6) Removable media transport logs with approvals.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Employ the principle of least functionality by configuring systems to provide only essential capabilities.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation describes disabling unnecessary services, ports, and protocols via Group Policy and Ansible automation, which directly addresses the requirement to configure systems for least functionality.

mappings.control_title: Remediate vulnerabilities in accordance with risk assessments.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation includes weekly Tenable.io scans, prioritization of vulnerabilities by severity, and ServiceNow-based remediation tracking with defined SLAs, satisfying the requirement to remediate based on risk assessments.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Inadequate Termination Procedures and Residual Sensor Access for Ex-Employees", "severity": "high", "description": "The organization lacks a documented process for revoking system access and disabling environmental sensors (e.g., cameras, microphones) upon employee termination. During an exit interview for a recently terminated system administrator, it was discovered that their badge, network credentials, and remote sensor control panel access remained active for three days post-termination. Additionally, the enterprise architecture does not include fail-safe procedures that automatically disable sensor capabilities when an account is deactivated.", "control_gaps": ["No formal checklist or automated workflow for terminating access upon employee departure", "Environmental sensors (e.g., smart cameras, audio devices) retain remote activation capabilities for terminated personnel", "Enterprise architecture lacks integration between HR termination triggers and system access controls", "No fail-safe mechanism to deactivate sensor access when a user account is disabled or expired"], "remediation": "Implement an automated termination workflow that triggers immediate revocation of all system and physical access, including environmental sensor controls. Update the enterprise architecture to require that sensor capabilities be centrally managed and tied to active user accounts. Define and deploy fail-safe procedures that automatically disable sensor access when a user account is terminated or expired."}

mappings.control_title: Personnel Termination
mappings.reasoning: The finding describes failure to terminate system access upon employee termination, directly addressing PS-4 which requires termination of information system access.

mappings.control_title: Sensor Capability and Data
mappings.reasoning: The finding highlights that terminated employees retained the ability to remotely activate environmental sensors (e.g., cameras, microphones), violating SC-42's prohibition on unauthorized remote activation of sensing capabilities.

mappings.control_title: Enterprise Architecture
mappings.reasoning: The finding notes that the enterprise architecture lacks integration between HR termination processes and system access controls, which is a core aspect of PM-7's requirement to develop an enterprise architecture with information security considerations.

mappings.control_title: Fail-Safe Procedures
mappings.reasoning: The absence of automatic deactivation of sensor access when an account is disabled represents a failure of fail-safe procedures as required by SI-17, which mandates that the system fails securely upon detection of a failure condition (e.g., account termination).

text: {"risk_id": "RISK-2025-001", "risk_statement": "Unencrypted Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) traffic could be intercepted by an attacker, potentially exposing Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) discussed during calls, and the shared network resources may allow unauthorized data transfer between VoIP and data systems.", "likelihood": "medium", "impact": "high", "inherent_risk": "high", "residual_risk": "medium", "treatment": "mitigate", "treatment_description": "Implement FIPS-validated encryption for all VoIP traffic, segment VoIP traffic onto a dedicated VLAN with strict access controls to prevent information leakage via shared resources, and deploy monitoring tools to detect unauthorized use or anomalies in VoIP activity."}

mappings.control_title: SC.L2-3.13.14 — Control and monitor the use of Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technologies.
mappings.reasoning: The risk directly addresses VoIP usage and the need to monitor such technologies to prevent interception and misuse, which aligns with this control.

mappings.control_title: SC.L2-3.13.4 — Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.
mappings.reasoning: The risk mentions shared network resources allowing unauthorized data transfer between VoIP and data systems, directly mapping to the requirement to prevent unintended information transfer via shared resources.

mappings.control_title: SC.L2-3.13.11 — Employ FIPS-validated cryptography when used to protect the confidentiality of CUI.
mappings.reasoning: The risk involves exposure of CUI due to unencrypted VoIP traffic, and the treatment recommends FIPS-validated encryption to protect confidentiality, which is the core requirement of this control.

mappings.control_title: SC.L2-3.13.11 — Employ FIPS-validated cryptography when used to protect the confidentiality of CUI.
mappings.reasoning: The risk statement explicitly mentions unencrypted VoIP traffic that could expose CUI, and the treatment plan emphasizes FIPS-validated cryptography, reinforcing the mapping to this control for data in transit.

text: {"implementation_id": "IMP-2025-03", "control_area": "Enterprise Data Protection and Continuity", "description": "The implementation covers physical and logical controls for media storage, water damage prevention, telecommunications redundancy, privacy notice compliance, and information sharing. Media is stored in a locked, fireproof cabinet with access logging and environmental monitoring for water leaks. A secondary internet connection with a failover agreement provides telecommunications resilience. The privacy notice is published on the intranet and public website, detailing PII processing, and information sharing is enabled via role-based access control (RBAC) with data classification tags.", "technologies": ["Locking server cabinets (e.g., APC NetShelter)", "Water leak detection sensors (e.g., Sensaphone)", "SD-WAN with failover (e.g., Cisco SD-WAN)", "Microsoft Azure Information Protection (AIP)", "OneTrust Privacy Management Platform"], "configuration_details": "Water leak sensors are placed under raised floors in data center zones A1\u2013A4, with alerts sent via SNMP to Nagios. SD-WAN policy sets primary circuit as fiber (500 Mbps) and secondary as LTE (100 Mbps) with 10-second failover. AIP labels are configured for 'Confidential' and 'Restricted' data; sharing is allowed only with explicit approval workflows in Microsoft Teams. Privacy notice is updated quarterly and hosted on a static HTML page with version control in Git.", "evidence": "Access logs from cabinet locks (e.g., Salto Systems) show user ID and timestamp for each media access. Weekly sensor test reports from Nagios confirm leak detection operational. SD-WAN failover logs demonstrate <15-second switchover during monthly tests. AIP audit logs show data sharing approvals. Privacy notice URL is included in employee onboarding documentation.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Media Storage
mappings.reasoning: The implementation uses locked cabinets and access logging for digital and non-digital media, directly addressing physical control and secure storage requirements of MP-4.

mappings.control_title: Water Damage Protection
mappings.reasoning: Water leak detection sensors and environmental monitoring protect against water damage, aligning with PE-15's requirement for protection from water leakage.

mappings.control_title: Telecommunications Services
mappings.reasoning: SD-WAN with secondary LTE circuit and failover agreement establishes alternate telecommunications services, meeting CP-8's requirement for continuity.

mappings.control_title: Privacy Notice
mappings.reasoning: The privacy notice is publicly available and describes PII processing, updated quarterly via Git, directly fulfilling PT-5's requirement for a privacy notice.

mappings.control_title: Information Sharing
mappings.reasoning: RBAC with AIP labels and approval workflows enables authorized information sharing based on defined circumstances, matching AC-21's information sharing requirement.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-001", "title": "Insufficient Backup Testing and Incident Analysis for System Availability", "current_state": "The organization performs daily backups of critical systems and data, but these backups are not regularly tested for restoration integrity. There is no formal process to analyze backup failures or related events for potential adverse impacts on system availability. Resource capacity planning is ad hoc and not linked to backup or incident data.", "target_state": "Backups must be created, protected, maintained, and tested per PR.DS-11. Potentially adverse events, including backup failures, must be analyzed to understand associated activities per DE.AE-02. Adequate resource capacity to ensure availability must be maintained per PR.IR-03, informed by backup and incident analysis.", "gap_description": "The current backup process lacks testing and integration with incident analysis and capacity planning, creating a risk that data restoration may fail and that resource availability is not assured based on known adverse events.", "recommended_actions": ["Implement quarterly restoration testing for all critical backups and document results.", "Establish a process to analyze backup failures and related events as potentially adverse events, including root cause analysis.", "Integrate backup test results and incident analysis data into capacity planning to ensure adequate resource capacity for availability.", "Develop and maintain a resource capacity plan that aligns with backup and incident metrics."], "priority": "high", "estimated_effort": "weeks"}

mappings.control_title: Backups of data are created, protected, maintained, and tested.
mappings.reasoning: The gap directly addresses the lack of backup testing, which is a core requirement of PR.DS-11 for maintaining and testing backups.

mappings.control_title: Potentially adverse events are analyzed to better understand associated activities.
mappings.reasoning: The gap highlights the absence of analysis for backup failures as potentially adverse events, which maps to DE.AE-02 requirements for event analysis.

mappings.control_title: Adequate resource capacity to ensure availability is maintained.
mappings.reasoning: The gap notes that resource capacity planning is ad hoc and not informed by backup or incident data, which is necessary to maintain adequate capacity per PR.IR-03.

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "System Configuration and Access Control Policy for Secure Facilities", "policy_text": "All organizational systems must maintain a documented baseline configuration, including hardware, software, and firmware components, with inventories updated quarterly. Physical access to server rooms and network closets shall be controlled via badge readers and logged automatically, with audit logs retained for at least one year. System security plans must be developed for all systems processing sensitive data and reviewed annually. Wireless access points within the facility must be configured with WPA3 encryption and authenticated using organizational credentials. Any changes to system baselines or physical access controls require prior approval from the Configuration Control Board.", "department": "Information Security and Compliance", "classification": "confidential"}

mappings.control_title: Establish and maintain baseline configurations and inventories of organizational systems.
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly requires documented baseline configurations, quarterly inventories of hardware, software, and firmware, directly aligning with CM.L2-3.4.1.

mappings.control_title: Control and manage physical access devices.
mappings.reasoning: The policy mandates physical access control via badge readers for server rooms and network closets, which directly addresses management of physical access devices.

mappings.control_title: Maintain audit logs of physical access.
mappings.reasoning: The requirement for automatic logging of physical access and retention for one year maps directly to maintaining audit logs of physical access.

mappings.control_title: Develop, document, and periodically update system security plans.
mappings.reasoning: The policy explicitly requires system security plans to be developed for systems processing sensitive data and reviewed annually, aligning with CA.L2-3.12.4.

mappings.control_title: Protect wireless access using authentication and encryption.
mappings.reasoning: The policy specifies WPA3 encryption and organizational credential authentication for wireless access, directly matching AC.L2-3.1.17.

mappings.control_title: Establish and maintain baseline configurations and inventories of organizational systems.
mappings.reasoning: The policy also references Configuration Control Board approval for changes, reinforcing baseline configuration management as per CM.L2-3.4.1.

text: {"finding_id": "F-2025-042", "title": "Uncontrolled Administrative Access and Configuration Drift in Production Environment", "severity": "high", "description": "During the audit, it was observed that several system administrators possess direct, unmonitored SSH access to production servers without documented approval or change control processes. Configuration changes are made ad-hoc, often without prior security impact analysis, and multiple non-essential services (e.g., FTP, Telnet) remain enabled on these systems. This lack of enforcement of access restrictions and absence of pre-implementation review increases the risk of unauthorized or unintended changes leading to security vulnerabilities.", "control_gaps": ["Physical and logical access restrictions for system changes are not defined, documented, or enforced for administrative access to production servers.", "Security impact analysis of changes is not performed prior to implementation, as evidenced by unapproved configuration changes.", "Principle of least functionality is violated by enabling non-essential services (FTP, Telnet) on production systems."], "remediation": "Implement a formal change management process that requires documented approval and security impact analysis for all configuration changes. Enforce role-based access controls with just-in-time privileges for administrative accounts, and disable all non-essential services on production systems. Regularly audit access logs and configurations to detect and remediate unauthorized changes."}

mappings.control_title: Define, document, approve, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes to organizational systems.
mappings.reasoning: The finding directly states that physical and logical access restrictions for system changes are not defined, documented, or enforced, which is the core requirement of CM.L2-3.4.5.

mappings.control_title: Analyze the security impact of changes prior to implementation.
mappings.reasoning: The finding highlights that changes are made without prior security impact analysis, explicitly mapping to the requirement of CM.L2-3.4.4.

mappings.control_title: Employ the principle of least functionality by configuring systems to provide only essential capabilities.
mappings.reasoning: The finding mentions non-essential services (FTP, Telnet) enabled on production systems, which violates the least functionality principle addressed by CM.L2-3.4.6.

mappings.control_title: Account Management
mappings.reasoning: The finding involves unmonitored administrative access and lack of documented approval, which relates to account management practices, though the primary focus is on change-related restrictions.

mappings.control_title: Least Privilege
mappings.reasoning: The direct administrative access without just-in-time or role-based controls suggests a violation of least privilege, which is partially covered by the finding.

mappings.control_title: Information System Component Inventory
mappings.reasoning: The presence of non-essential services implies a lack of proper configuration management and inventory, but the finding does not directly address inventory tracking.

text: {"implementation_id": "impl-ma-2024-001", "control_area": "Maintenance", "description": "The organization implements a centralized maintenance management system (ServiceNow) to control all system maintenance activities. All maintenance personnel, including third-party vendors, must be vetted and approved through an identity management workflow (SailPoint) before being granted access to perform maintenance. Nonlocal maintenance sessions are conducted over encrypted VPN tunnels (Palo Alto VPN) and are monitored and recorded using a privileged access management solution (BeyondTrust). All maintenance actions, whether local or remote, are logged in ServiceNow with detailed records, including the technician, date, duration, and actions performed, and these logs are reviewed weekly by the security team.", "technologies": ["ServiceNow (ITSM)", "SailPoint IdentityNow", "BeyondTrust (PAM)", "Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN", "Splunk (SIEM for log aggregation)"], "configuration_details": "ServiceNow: Maintenance requests require approval from system owner and security manager. SailPoint: Access certifications run quarterly for maintenance accounts. BeyondTrust: Session recording enabled for all nonlocal maintenance, with approval required before session start. Palo Alto VPN: Split tunneling disabled; all maintenance traffic routed through corporate firewall. Splunk: Indexes maintenance logs from ServiceNow and BeyondTrust with retention of 1 year.", "evidence": "Weekly review reports of maintenance logs in Splunk; quarterly access certification reports from SailPoint; session recording archives from BeyondTrust; ServiceNow change tickets with approval history.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Maintenance Personnel
mappings.reasoning: The implementation describes a process for vetting and approving maintenance personnel through SailPoint identity management, which directly supports MA-5 requirements for authorization and maintaining a list of authorized personnel.

mappings.control_title: Nonlocal Maintenance
mappings.reasoning: The use of BeyondTrust for session monitoring/recording, Palo Alto VPN for encrypted remote access, and approval workflows in ServiceNow directly map to MA-4 requirements for approving and monitoring nonlocal maintenance activities.

mappings.control_title: Controlled Maintenance
mappings.reasoning: The scheduled, documented maintenance process in ServiceNow with weekly review of logs maps to MA-2 requirements for scheduling, performing, documenting, and reviewing maintenance records.

mappings.control_title: Content of Audit Records
mappings.reasoning: The detailed logging of technician, date, duration, and actions in ServiceNow and BeyondTrust aligns with AU-3 requirements for sufficient audit record content, as maintenance logs include necessary details.

mappings.control_title: Least Privilege
mappings.reasoning: The approval workflows and access certifications in SailPoint and ServiceNow ensure maintenance personnel only have the minimum access needed for their tasks, supporting the least privilege principle outlined in AC-6.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-2024-0042", "title": "Inadequate Email Security and Remote Access Controls", "current_state": "The organization uses a basic email filtering solution that only blocks known malware attachments and obvious spam. Remote access is permitted via VPN without multi-factor authentication, and there are no restrictions on unsuccessful logon attempts. Cyber threat intelligence is collected from a single open-source feed without formal evaluation processes.", "target_state": "The organization should implement a comprehensive spam protection mechanism that filters malicious content, phishing attempts, and unwanted messages. Remote access lacks monitoring and control capabilities, and there are no limits on failed logon attempts. mappings.control_title: Limit unsuccessful logon attempts

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Incident Eradication and Resiliency Communication Policy", "policy_text": "All incidents must be eradicated through approved containment and removal procedures, with redundant critical systems activated as necessary to maintain operations. mappings.control_title: Redundancy for critical systems is implemented based on risk
mappings.reasoning: The policy states that redundant critical systems are activated as necessary to maintain operations, referencing risk-based implementation of redundancy, which maps to PR.IR-05.

mappings.control_title: Lines of communication across the organization are established for cybersecurity risk management

text: {"implementation_id": "impl-009", "control_area": "Access Control, Audit, Risk Assessment, and System Communications", "description": "In this enterprise environment, all network access to privileged and non-privileged accounts is secured using Kerberos authentication from Active Directory, which inherently provides replay-resistant tickets (timestamps and session keys). Audit logs from domain controllers, firewalls, and servers are written to a centralized SIEM (Splunk) with role-based access controls and write-once storage to prevent tampering. Confidential CUI at rest is encrypted using BitLocker full-disk encryption with TPM-based key protection and AES-256, and file shares use EFS or SMB encryption. Cyber threat intelligence sources are aggregated via an automated feed from Recorded Future and integrated into the SIEM for correlation and alerting, with a monthly evaluation process documented in the risk register.", "technologies": ["Microsoft Active Directory (Kerberos)", "Splunk Enterprise Security", "BitLocker Drive Encryption", "Recorded Future Threat Intelligence", "Windows Server 2019/2022", "Group Policy Management"], "configuration_details": "Kerberos ticket lifetime set to 10 hours for users, 4 hours for privileged accounts; BitLocker uses AES-256 with 128-bit diffuser, TPM+PIN for authentication; Splunk audit logs retained for 1 year with SHA-256 hashing for integrity; Recorded Future feeds ingested via API every 15 minutes into Splunk TA-recordedfuture.", "evidence": "Active Directory security policy showing Kerberos settings; BitLocker recovery key backups in Active Directory; Splunk audit log retention policy and access logs; Recorded Future subscription and integration test results; monthly threat intelligence evaluation reports.", "status": "implemented"}

mappings.control_title: Employ replay-resistant authentication mechanisms for network access to privileged and non-privileged accounts.
mappings.reasoning: The implementation uses Kerberos authentication, which by design employs timestamps and session keys to prevent replay attacks, covering both privileged and non-privileged network access.

mappings.control_title: Protect audit information and audit logging tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion.
mappings.reasoning: Audit logs are sent to Splunk with write-once storage, role-based access controls, and SHA-256 hashing to ensure integrity and prevent unauthorized modification or deletion.

mappings.control_title: Protect the confidentiality of CUI at rest.
mappings.reasoning: Confidential CUI at rest is protected using BitLocker full-disk encryption with AES-256 and TPM-based key protection, which directly addresses the requirement for confidentiality of data at rest.

mappings.control_title: Employ a means for evaluating cyber threat intelligence sources.
mappings.reasoning: The organization uses Recorded Future threat intelligence feeds integrated into Splunk, with a monthly evaluation process documented in the risk register, providing a systematic means to evaluate threat intelligence sources.

text: {"gap_id": "GAP-2025-001", "title": "Lack of Formalized Recovery, Behavior, and Policy Documentation for IT Systems", "current_state": "The organization has ad-hoc recovery procedures documented in informal email threads and shared drives but lacks a formal, approved recovery and reconstitution plan. Identifier management for system accounts is tracked via spreadsheets without a standardized authorization process.", "target_state": "A documented and approved recovery and reconstitution plan (CP-10) must exist and be tested to restore systems to a known state. Identifier management (IA-4) must include a formal authorization process for issuing and tracking system identifiers.", "gap_description": "The current state lacks formal policy documentation, approval, and dissemination for recovery, behavior, risk assessment, incident response, and identifier management, creating significant compliance and operational risks.", "recommended_actions": ["Develop and approve a formal system recovery and reconstitution plan per CP-10", "Create and distribute a written rules of behavior document for all employees", "Formally document and approve risk assessment policy and procedures per RA-1", "Develop and disseminate incident response policy and procedures per IR-1", "Establish a formal identifier management process with authorization tracking per IA-4"], "priority": "high", "estimated_effort": "months"}

mappings.control_title: Policy and Procedures

text: {"document_type": "policy", "title": "Incident Escalation and Resource Capacity Policy", "policy_text": "All security incidents must be assessed for impact and scope within one hour of detection, and escalated to the appropriate incident response team if they exceed a predefined severity threshold.
